name: Joni Eareckson Tada

significant_dates.details: Diving accident in Chesapeake Bay caused permanent quadriplegia
Founded Joni and Friends disability ministry
Married Ken Tada

source_summary: Joni Eareckson Tada (born 1949) is an American Christian author, artist, and disability advocate. At age 17 she became a quadriplegic following a diving accident in the Chesapeake Bay in July 1967. After a period of intense struggle with depression and despair, she emerged with a deepened Christian faith. She founded Joni and Friends in 1979, a ministry serving people with disabilities and their families worldwide. She has authored over fifty books and paints holding a brush in her mouth. She has also survived breast cancer and continues in ministry.
adult_story:
Joni Eareckson Tada was born in 1949 in Baltimore, Maryland. In July 1967, at the age of 17, she dived into the Chesapeake Bay and struck the bottom — breaking her neck and leaving her a quadriplegic.

The years that followed were among the darkest of her life. She wrestled with depression, rage, and a desire to die. She was sustained by friends who refused to leave, by Scripture, and by a slow, painful re-engagement with a God whose purposes she could not yet see. She emerged not with easy answers, but with a conviction — deepened across six decades — that suffering is not evidence of God's absence but the terrain on which he is most clearly known.

She founded Joni and Friends in 1979, a ministry now active worldwide serving people with disabilities and their families. She has authored over fifty books, recorded music, and painted with a brush held in her mouth. She has also endured breast cancer and chronic pain.

She has said publicly that heaven is more real to her than her wheelchair — not as an escape from suffering, but as the resolution of a story she believes God is still writing.
family_story:
When Joni Eareckson Tada was 17 years old, she dived into a lake, hit the bottom, and broke her neck. She has been in a wheelchair ever since, unable to use her hands.

At first she was angry and heartbroken. But slowly, through reading the Bible and praying, she came to trust that God had not abandoned her — and that her life still had meaning.

She started Joni and Friends, a ministry helping people with disabilities worldwide. She paints pictures holding a brush in her mouth, and has written over fifty books.

Joni says heaven feels more real than her wheelchair — not because she wants to escape, but because she believes God is writing a story that will one day make sense of everything.

name: Dwight L. Moody

significant_dates.details: Converted to Christianity in Boston after Edward Kimball spoke with him of faith
Married Emma C. Revell
Founded the Illinois Street Church in Chicago

Died in Northfield, Massachusetts

source_summary: Dwight L. Moody (1837–1899) was an American non-denominational evangelist and educator. Born in poverty in Northfield, Massachusetts, his father died when he was four. He converted at eighteen in Boston through the witness of his Sunday school teacher Edward Kimball. Moving to Chicago, he founded what became Moody Church and, with singer Ira Sankey, conducted mass evangelistic campaigns across Britain and North America in the 1870s and 1880s. He founded Northfield Schools and in 1886 established what became Moody Bible Institute. It is estimated he preached to more than one hundred million people in his lifetime.
adult_story:
Dwight Lyman Moody was born in 1837 in Northfield, Massachusetts, the sixth of nine children. His father died when Dwight was four, leaving the family in poverty. He had little formal education and moved to Boston at seventeen to work in a shoe store.

His employer's condition was that he attend Sunday school. It was there that his teacher Edward Kimball sought him out in the back room of the shop in 1855 and asked if he was willing to give his life to Christ. Moody was. It was, as he later described it, the day everything changed.

He moved to Chicago, prospered in business, and might have remained prosperous — but in 1860 he gave it up for full-time ministry among the poor of the city. He built a Sunday school in the slums that grew into what became Moody Church. He partnered with singer [[Ira Sankey|ira-sankey]], and together they conducted evangelistic campaigns across Britain and North America through the 1870s and 1880s that drew hundreds of thousands.

He founded Northfield schools in Massachusetts, and in 1886 established the school that became Moody Bible Institute — training ordinary men and women for ministry without requiring the formal education he himself had never received.

He died in 1899. It is estimated he preached to more than one hundred million people — each one brought face to face with a God who, as Moody put it, is not angry but loves them.
family_story:
Dwight L. Moody grew up very poor in Massachusetts after his father died when he was just four years old. At seventeen he moved to Boston to work in a shoe shop.

His boss made him go to Sunday school. One day his teacher came to the shop and asked Dwight in the back room if he was ready to give his life to Jesus. He said yes — and nothing was ever the same.

Moody moved to Chicago, gave up a good business career, and spent the rest of his life telling people that God loved them. He preached to huge crowds across America and Britain, and started a school to train preachers who couldn't afford a university education.

He showed that God uses ordinary people to do extraordinary things.

name: W. E. Vine

significant_dates.details: Married Phoebe Baxendale
Received BA and MA in Ancient Classics from University of London
Published Expository Dictionary of New Testament Words

source_summary: William Edwy Vine (1873–1949) was a British Open Brethren Bible teacher and author based in Bath, Somerset. He studied at University College, Bristol, and devoted his career to teaching, preaching, and writing in Brethren assemblies. His principal work, Vine's Expository Dictionary of New Testament Words, published in four volumes between 1939 and 1941, traces the Greek roots of New Testament vocabulary in plain English, making original-language scholarship accessible to preachers and lay readers without formal Greek training.
adult_story:
William Edwy Vine was born in 1873 in Somerset and educated at University College, Bristol. He spent his entire adult life in Open Brethren assemblies — teaching, preaching, and writing in the conviction that Scripture, rightly understood, was the church's sufficient guide.

He is best known for Vine's Expository Dictionary of New Testament Words, published in four volumes between 1939 and 1941. The Dictionary traces every significant word in the Greek New Testament back to its root, explaining its range of meaning in careful, plain English. For the first time, preachers and teachers without formal Greek training could access the depth of the original text — the difference between agapē and philia, between justification and righteousness, between knowledge that merely informs and wisdom that transforms.

Vine wrote it in the conviction that the words of Scripture were God-breathed in every detail, and that careful attention to them was an act of worship as much as an act of study. He completed the work in his late sixties, seeing it through to publication near the end of his life.

His name is not widely celebrated. But Vine's Dictionary sits on the shelves of preachers, teachers, and careful Bible readers around the world — a quiet instrument, still doing what he intended: placing the wealth of the New Testament's words within reach of those who love them.
family_story:
W.E. Vine was an English Bible teacher who spent his life helping ordinary Christians understand the New Testament more clearly.

His most famous book is Vine's Expository Dictionary of New Testament Words. It explains what the important Greek words in the New Testament really mean — because the New Testament was written in Greek, and sometimes a translation can't capture the full depth of the original.

He believed every Christian, not just scholars, deserved to understand what God's word actually said. Thousands of pastors and teachers all over the world still use his Dictionary today.

He showed that careful attention to Scripture is one of the most important ways we can serve the church — and that you don't have to be famous to leave something lasting.

name: James Strong

Became Professor of Exegetical Theology at Drew Theological Seminary

Died in Round Lake, New York

source_summary: James Strong (1822–1894) was an American Methodist biblical scholar and professor of exegetical theology at Drew Theological Seminary, New Jersey. After more than thirty years of work with a team of scholars, he published Strong's Exhaustive Concordance of the Bible in 1890 — the most comprehensive concordance of the King James Bible ever compiled. The concordance cross-references every word with a Hebrew and Greek lexicon using a numbering system ('Strong's numbers') that remains the standard reference in biblical word study more than 130 years later.
adult_story:
James Strong was born in New York City in 1822. After studies in classics and theology he served for many years as professor of exegetical theology at Drew Theological Seminary in New Jersey, and as a Methodist churchman devoted to the careful study of Scripture.

In 1890, after more than thirty years of labour, he published his Exhaustive Concordance of the Bible. Every word in the King James Bible is indexed, and each entry is linked by number to its original Hebrew or Greek term in a companion lexicon. The work was compiled by hand, before computers — a feat of sustained scholarly patience by Strong and his team that took decades to complete.

Strong's numbers, as they came to be known, became a standard reference tool that remains in daily use more than 130 years later. A preacher with no knowledge of biblical languages can look up any English word, find its number, and trace it back to its root in the original text — a doorway Strong spent his life building and leaving open.

He died in 1894. The Concordance outlasted him and is still published today, probably the most widely used Bible study reference in the English-speaking world.
family_story:
James Strong was an American professor who spent over thirty years building one of the most useful Bible study tools ever made.

His book — Strong's Exhaustive Concordance — lists every single word in the King James Bible, tells you exactly where to find it, and links it to the original Hebrew or Greek word it was translated from. He did it all by hand, with a team of helpers, with no computers.

His numbering system — Strong's numbers — is still used in Bible study apps and books today, more than 130 years later.

He showed that loving the Bible means being willing to work hard to understand it — and that careful, patient scholarship is its own kind of service to the church.

name: Alexander Cruden

significant_dates.details: Presented first edition of his Bible Concordance to Queen Caroline
Presented second edition of Concordance to King George III
Published third edition of the Concordance
Died while praying in his lodgings, Islington

source_summary: Alexander Cruden (1699–1770) was a Scottish Presbyterian bookseller, proofreader, and biblical scholar who lived and worked in London. He compiled his Complete Concordance to the Holy Scriptures largely alone, publishing the first edition in 1737 — the first major concordance of the entire English Bible. He revised it twice more; the 1769 edition remains in print. Cruden experienced significant personal instability, including three commitments to private asylums, but was earnest in his Calvinist Presbyterian faith and devoted to making Scripture accessible to ordinary readers.
adult_story:
Alexander Cruden was born in Aberdeen in 1699, educated at Marischal College, and came to London in his twenties where he worked as a bookseller and proofreader. He was a committed Calvinist Presbyterian whose personal life was marked by grief, loneliness, and several breakdowns — episodes that led to his brief commitments to private asylums, events his enemies mocked and his friends mourned.

In 1737 he published the first edition of his Complete Concordance to the Holy Scriptures, having compiled it largely alone over years of painstaking work. It was the first major concordance of the entire English Bible: every significant word indexed by chapter and verse, so that any reader could locate any passage by a word they remembered. He dedicated it to Queen Caroline. He revised and expanded it in 1761 and 1769; the final edition remains in print.

In his later years he styled himself "Alexander the Corrector" and mounted a self-appointed campaign for national moral reform. He was eccentric, sometimes erratic — but he was earnest. He prayed over every page of his Concordance and regarded his life's work as a gift to the church.

His Concordance outlasted his eccentricities. For nearly three centuries it has been placing the words of Scripture within reach of those who need to find them.
family_story:
Alexander Cruden was a Scottish bookseller in London who spent years doing one of the most useful things anyone has ever done for Bible readers.

He compiled the first big concordance of the whole English Bible — a book that lists every important word in Scripture and tells you exactly where to find it. He did most of the work himself, alone, over many years.

His life had some very hard times — he struggled with his health and with loneliness, and people sometimes mocked him. But he kept working, because he believed God's word deserved to be findable by anyone.

The concordance he made — Cruden's Concordance — has been in print for nearly 300 years. Millions of people have used it to find the passages they needed.

name: William MacDonald

significant_dates.details: Became President of Emmaus Bible College
Left presidency to begin international Bible-teaching ministry
Published Believer's Bible Commentary

source_summary: William MacDonald (1917–2007) was an American Plymouth Brethren teacher and author. He served as president of Emmaus Bible College in Oak Park, Illinois (1959–1965) before pursuing a wider Bible-teaching ministry across the United States, Europe, and Asia. He published more than eighty books, above all the Believer's Bible Commentary (1989), a single-volume exposition of every book of Scripture widely used among evangelical Christians.
adult_story:
William MacDonald was born on 7 January 1917 in Leominster, Massachusetts. After earning degrees from Tufts College and Harvard Business School, he worked as an investment analyst at the First National Bank of Boston — but commerce gave way to conviction. He served in the Navy during the Second World War, and afterwards gave himself entirely to Bible teaching through the Plymouth Brethren.

From 1946 he served on the faculty of Emmaus Bible School in Oak Park, Illinois, becoming its president in 1959. After stepping down in 1965, he spent years teaching in the United States, Europe, and Asia, then joined the Discipleship Intern Training Program in California, which he served until 1996.

MacDonald published more than eighty books, but his lasting monument is the *Believer's Bible Commentary* (1989) — a single-volume exposition of every book of Scripture, written in plain and accessible prose. Where figures such as [[William E. Vine|william-edwy-vine]] had mapped the language of the New Testament, MacDonald mapped the whole landscape of the Bible for the ordinary believer. His *True Discipleship* pressed the saved believer beyond nominal religion into whole-hearted allegiance — not confusing the cost of discipleship with the free gift of salvation, but refusing to accept that a faith with no fruit in one's life was saving faith at all.

He died on 25 December 2007, Christmas Day, having spent sixty years making the Word of Christ dwell richly in whoever would read.
family_story:
William MacDonald was an American teacher who believed the Bible was the most important book ever written — and spent his life helping ordinary people understand it.

After studying at some of America's most famous universities, he gave up a career in banking to teach God's Word full-time. He led Emmaus Bible College and wrote more than eighty books.

His biggest book, the *Believer's Bible Commentary*, goes through every book of the Bible and explains what it means. Millions of Christians have used it to understand Scripture better.

Bill MacDonald also wrote about what it truly means to follow Jesus — not just in words, but with your whole life. He spent sixty years helping others trust in God's grace.

source_summary: Henry Allan 'Harry' Ironside (1876–1951) was a Canadian-American Bible teacher, preacher, and pastor who served Moody Church in Chicago from 1929 to 1948. Born in Toronto and raised in Los Angeles, he preached his first sermons as a child and was converted at thirteen. He served in the Salvation Army as a teenager, later joined the Plymouth Brethren, and from 1916 to 1929 preached nearly 7,000 sermons to over 1.25 million listeners. He published more than 100 books and was known as 'the Archbishop of Fundamentalism.'
adult_story:
Henry Allan Ironside was born in Toronto in 1876 and raised by his widowed mother after his father died of typhoid when Harry was just two. The family moved to Los Angeles in 1886, and at eleven years old Harry gathered neighbourhood children under a canvas tent and taught them the Bible himself, averaging sixty each session. In 1888 he heard [[Dwight L. Moody|dwight-l-moody]] preach to eight thousand people — and resolved to do the same. After months wrestling with whether he was truly born again, he trusted Christ at thirteen. 'I rested on the Word of God and confessed Christ as my Saviour,' he said.

By sixteen he was a full-time Salvation Army preacher delivering four hundred sermons a year. He later joined the Plymouth Brethren, moving in the same world as [[George Müller|george-muller]], and from 1916 preached nearly seven thousand sermons to more than a million and a quarter listeners.

In 1929 he was called to pastor Moody Church in Chicago — the church built in the tradition of [[Dwight L. Moody|dwight-l-moody]] — where he served for nineteen years, filling its four-thousand-seat auditorium Sunday after Sunday. He served as president of the Africa Inland Mission from 1942.

Ironside published more than a hundred books, commentaries on virtually every book of the Bible, written with the clarity of a man who had left school after the eighth grade but never stopped learning from Scripture. He died in New Zealand in January 1951, still mid-tour, still bearing witness.
family_story:
Harry Ironside grew up without a father — his dad died when Harry was just two. When his family moved to Los Angeles, eleven-year-old Harry could not find a Sunday school. So he started his own, sewing old sacks into a tent — and up to sixty children came each week.

At thirteen, Harry trusted in Jesus after months of wondering whether he was truly saved. From that day he never stopped telling others about Christ. By sixteen he was a full-time preacher.

Harry eventually pastored Moody Church in Chicago — a building that held four thousand people — and wrote more than a hundred Bible books. He left school after eighth grade, but he never stopped learning from God's Word.

name: John Wycliffe

significant_dates.details: Birth year disputed; sources suggest mid-1320s

source_summary: John Wycliffe (c.1328–1384) was an English theologian and professor at the University of Oxford, called the ‘Morning Star of the Reformation.’ He challenged papal authority, denounced clerical wealth, and championed Scripture as the supreme authority of the church over a century before Luther. Rector of Lutterworth from 1374, he initiated the first translation of the Bible into Middle English. In 1377 he was summoned before the Archbishop of Canterbury at St Paul’s Cathedral in London; the proceedings ended in disorder. His writings directly shaped Jan Hus, and through Hus the Protestant Reformation. The Council of Constance condemned him posthumously in 1415; in 1428 his bones were exhumed and burned, his ashes cast into the River Swift at Lutterworth.
adult_story:
John Wycliffe was born around 1328 in Yorkshire and became one of Oxford’s foremost scholars — a philosopher, theologian, and doctor of divinity. He became rector of Lutterworth in Leicestershire in 1374, a post he held until his death.

He is called the “Morning Star of the Reformation” for the convictions he held a century before Luther: that Scripture holds supreme authority over church and pope, that salvation rests on God’s grace and election rather than on the church’s sacramental system, and that every person deserves to hear God’s Word in their own language. He challenged the doctrine of transubstantiation and denounced clerical corruption openly. In 1377 he was summoned before the Archbishop of Canterbury at St Paul’s Cathedral in London — the proceedings collapsed in disorder, and he returned to Lutterworth unmolested.

His most enduring legacy was to begin translating the Bible into Middle English. His writings directly shaped [[John Hus|john-hus]], and through Hus the whole Protestant Reformation. [[William Tyndale|william-tyndale]], who completed the first printed English New Testament, followed a path Wycliffe had opened.

The Council of Constance condemned him posthumously in 1415. In 1428 his bones were exhumed and burned, his ashes cast into the River Swift. As the river carried his ashes to the sea, so his doctrine spread across the world.
family_story:
John Wycliffe lived in England about 650 years ago. He was a professor at Oxford University and a village priest in Lutterworth.

John believed the Bible — not the Pope — should be the highest authority. He translated it into English so that ordinary people could read God’s Word for the first time.

Church leaders were furious. He was summoned to London to stand trial — but stood firm and came home safely. After he died, his enemies dug up his bones and burned them, throwing his ashes into the River Swift.

But his ideas spread like his ashes across rivers and seas — and lit the fire of the Reformation.

name: Charlotte Elliott

significant_dates.details: Born at Westfield Lodge, Brighton, England
Found peace with God after counsel from Rev. César Malan, who told her to come 'just as she was'
Wrote the hymn 'Just As I Am, Without One Plea'
Died in Brighton, England

hymns: Just As I Am, Without One Plea
My God and Father While I Stray
source_summary: Charlotte Elliott (1789–1871) was an English evangelical Anglican hymn writer born in Brighton, granddaughter of Henry Venn of the Clapham Sect. After a spiritually empty early life she came to faith in 1822 through Swiss evangelist César Malan, who told her to 'come just as you are.' From 1829 she suffered chronic illness and spent most of her life confined to her room, yet edited The Invalid's Hymn Book and wrote approximately 150 hymns. Her most famous, 'Just As I Am, Without One Plea' (1836), became the defining invitation hymn of evangelical crusades and was used by Billy Graham at virtually every meeting he ever led.
adult_story:
Charlotte Elliott was born in Brighton in 1789, granddaughter of Henry Venn of the Clapham Sect and raised among evangelical believers. For her first thirty years she lived a cultured but spiritually empty life. A serious illness in 1821 unsettled her, and in May 1822 a Swiss evangelist named [[César Malan|cesar-malan]] visited her father's home and asked whether she had made her peace with God. Elliott resented the question — but it would not leave her. Days later she sought Malan out. When she asked how to come to Christ, he said simply: “Come just as you are.” She trusted Christ that day, marking 9 May 1822 as the birthday of her soul.

From 1829 Charlotte suffered chronic illness that largely confined her to her room for decades. She might have become bitter; instead she edited The Invalid's Hymn Book and wrote some 150 hymns — most anonymously — convinced that a bedridden saint could still serve Christ through words. In 1836 she wrote ‘Just As I Am, Without One Plea.’ Its stanzas strip away every human condition for approaching God and rest the soul entirely on the blood of Christ. Hymnologist Kenneth Osbeck called it the hymn that had “touched more hearts and influenced more people for Christ than any other song.” [[Billy Graham|billy-graham]] used it at virtually every evangelistic crusade he ever led.

Charlotte Elliott brought nothing to God but her need. That was exactly what the gospel had always asked.
family_story:
Charlotte Elliott grew up in Brighton, England, but didn't trust Jesus herself. When she was thirty-two, a pastor named [[César Malan|cesar-malan]] asked her: “Have you made your peace with God?” Charlotte was upset at first — but the question wouldn't leave her. When she went back and asked how to come to God, he said: “Just come as you are.” She believed in Jesus that day.

Later, Charlotte became very ill and spent most of her life in bed. Instead of giving up, she wrote hymns. Her most famous is ‘Just As I Am’ — a song about coming to Jesus with nothing to offer but your need. [[Billy Graham|billy-graham]] used it to invite people to trust Christ at every meeting he ever led.

name: César Malan

significant_dates.details: Born in Geneva

source_summary: Henri Abraham César Malan (1787–1864) was a Swiss Reformed minister and hymn writer, born in Geneva. Ordained in 1810, he was transformed by Robert Haldane's 1816–1817 teaching on Romans in Geneva, which sparked the Réveil revival movement. Suspended in 1818 and formally defrocked in 1823 for preaching justification by faith alone, he founded an independent chapel, l'Église du témoignage, in his own garden and preached there for forty years. He made nine journeys to Britain and was known for personal evangelistic conversations. In May 1822 he spoke with Charlotte Elliott in Brighton and told her to 'come just as you are' — words she later transformed into the hymn 'Just As I Am, Without One Plea.'
adult_story:
César Malan was born in Geneva in 1787 and ordained as a Reformed minister in 1810. His early preaching was conventional, but in 1816 Scottish evangelist Robert Haldane came to Geneva and taught a group of young ministers through the letter to the Romans. Malan emerged from those studies gripped by justification by faith alone — and could not stop preaching it.

In 1817 a sermon on the new birth brought his first suspension. Restored, he preached the same again and was formally defrocked in 1823. He responded by founding l'Église du témoignage — the Church of Testimony — in his own garden, where he preached for forty years. He made nine journeys to Britain and was known for personal conversations about the soul's need of Christ.

One such conversation, in May 1822, changed English hymnody. At a dinner in Brighton, Malan spoke of being useful to God to a young woman named [[Charlotte Elliott|charlotte-elliott]]. She resented the remark. Days later she sought him out and asked how she might come to Christ. He told her simply: “Come just as you are.” She trusted Christ that day. Fourteen years later she turned those words into the hymn ‘Just As I Am, Without One Plea.’

Malan was defrocked for preaching that salvation is by faith alone. The words he spoke to Charlotte Elliott proved him right.
family_story:
César Malan was a pastor in Geneva, Switzerland, who got into serious trouble for telling the truth. He believed people are saved not by being good, but by trusting in Jesus alone — and he preached this so boldly that church leaders fired him.

Instead of giving up, Malan built a little chapel in his garden and kept preaching for the rest of his life.

One day in England in 1822, he met a young woman named [[Charlotte Elliott|charlotte-elliott]]. She asked how she could come to God. He said: “Just come as you are.” She trusted Jesus that day — and fourteen years later she wrote the hymn ‘Just As I Am,’ sung by millions ever since.

name: Billy Graham

significant_dates.details: Born on a dairy farm near Charlotte, North Carolina.
Converted at revival meetings led by evangelist Mordecai Ham in Charlotte.
Drew up the Modesto Manifesto, an integrity code widely adopted across evangelistic ministry.
Los Angeles tent crusade brought him to national prominence.
Founded the Billy Graham Evangelistic Association.
Died at his home in Montreat, North Carolina, aged 99.

footnote: In a 1997 televised interview with Robert Schuller, Graham suggested that people who have never explicitly heard of or believed in Christ might nonetheless be saved through him unknowingly — an inclusivist remark that drew criticism from conservative evangelicals for softening the New Testament's call to explicit, conscious faith in Christ. It stands apart from his lifelong preaching, which consistently proclaimed a grace-alone gospel and called hearers to a personal, explicit decision for Christ. He was also criticized by some fundamentalist Protestants for cooperating with Roman Catholic and mainline clergy on local crusade organizing committees, a practice he defended as sending converts back to whatever church they already belonged to rather than building his own following.
source_summary: Billy Graham (1918-2018) was an American Southern Baptist evangelist whose crusades, radio and television broadcasts, and founding of the Billy Graham Evangelistic Association made him one of the most widely heard preachers in Christian history. Born near Charlotte, North Carolina, he was converted at sixteen at a revival led by Mordecai Ham. He rose to national prominence through his 1949 Los Angeles crusade, later founding BGEA (1950), Christianity Today (1956), and World Wide Pictures. He insisted on racially integrated crusades from the 1950s, was close to numerous US presidents, and preached live to an estimated 210 million people in over 185 countries across six decades of ministry before his death in 2018.
adult_story:
William Franklin Graham Jr. was born in 1918 on a dairy farm near Charlotte, North Carolina, and raised in a Presbyterian church. At sixteen he was converted at revival meetings led by evangelist Mordecai Ham, later trained at Wheaton College, and was ordained a Southern Baptist minister in 1939.

In 1948 Graham and his team drew up the Modesto Manifesto, a code of financial and personal integrity that became a model across evangelistic ministry. His 1949 Los Angeles tent crusade made him a national figure, and in 1950 he founded the Billy Graham Evangelistic Association, through which he pioneered evangelism by radio, television, and film, and helped launch Christianity Today magazine. Over six decades of crusades he preached the gospel face to face to more people than anyone before him, continuing the altar call practice begun by [[Dwight L. Moody|dwight-l-moody]], and always closing with [[Charlotte Elliott|charlotte-elliott]]'s hymn "Just As I Am."

From the 1950s Graham insisted his crusades be racially integrated at a time when much of America was not. At a 1953 rally in Chattanooga, Tennessee, he tore down the ropes organizers had strung to separate the races, telling ushers to leave them down "or you can go on and have the revival without me." For Graham it was simply the gospel worked out: salvation is a gift held out to anyone who will receive it by faith, without regard to race or merit, because Christ's death paid a debt none of us could pay ourselves.
family_story:
Billy Graham grew up on a dairy farm in North Carolina. When he was sixteen, he heard a traveling preacher and gave his life to Jesus — and he never turned back.

Billy became a preacher too, but he didn't just preach in one church. He used tents, radio, and even television to tell millions of people around the world about Jesus. More people heard him preach in person than anyone else in history.

Billy believed everyone was equal in God's eyes. At one meeting in the 1950s, organizers wanted to keep Black and white listeners apart. Billy pulled down the ropes separating them himself. He wanted everyone to know Jesus died for anyone who believes in him — no exceptions.

name: Harriet Tubman

significant_dates.details: Born Araminta Ross into slavery in Dorchester County, Maryland.
Suffered a severe head injury from an overseer, triggering lifelong visions she took as messages from God.
Escaped slavery alone, walking roughly ninety miles to freedom in Pennsylvania.
Led the Combahee River Raid, freeing more than 700 enslaved people.
Died in Auburn, New York, aged about 91.

footnote: Tubman interpreted the vivid visions and premonitions that followed a childhood head injury as direct revelations from God, and her contemporaries and biographers record these experiences as central to her sense of calling. This kind of ongoing personal revelation is not universally accepted across evangelical traditions, though her lifelong trust in Christ and reliance on prayer are not in question.
source_summary: Harriet Tubman (c. 1822–1913) was an American abolitionist born into slavery in Maryland. After escaping in 1849, she returned to slave-holding territory roughly thirteen times over the next decade, personally leading about seventy enslaved people to freedom via the Underground Railroad, earning the nickname "Moses." During the Civil War she served the Union Army as a scout, spy, and nurse, and in 1863 led the armed raid on the Combahee River that freed more than 700 enslaved people — the first woman known to lead a US military operation. Raised Methodist, she attributed her courage and guidance to visions she believed came from God. She spent her later years in Auburn, New York, caring for the elderly poor, and was active in the women's suffrage movement.
adult_story:
Harriet Tubman was born Araminta Ross into slavery around 1822 in Dorchester County, Maryland. As a teenager, a blow from an overseer left her with lifelong seizures and, she believed, visions from God. Raised in her enslaved parents' Methodist faith, she escaped alone in 1849, walking ninety miles to freedom in Pennsylvania.

She became known as "Moses." Over the next decade she returned south thirteen times, leading about seventy enslaved people — including her parents — north along the Underground Railroad, and never lost one. She later said, "God set the North Star in the heavens; he gave me the strength in my limbs; he meant I should be free." In the Civil War she served as a Union scout and nurse, and in June 1863 guided an armed raid freeing more than seven hundred enslaved people on the Combahee River.

Tubman credited none of it to her courage. Thomas Garrett, the Quaker abolitionist who worked alongside her, said he had "never met with any person of any color who had more confidence in the voice of God, as spoken direct to her soul." The freedom she risked her life for pointed to a freedom already hers as a gift — salvation held out not to the deserving but to anyone who trusts Christ. In old age she opened her Auburn, New York home to the elderly poor and worshipped at Thompson AME Zion Church, where she was buried in 1913 beneath the words "Servant of God, Well Done."
family_story:
Harriet Tubman was born into slavery in Maryland around 1822. As a girl a cruel overseer badly hurt her, and afterward she had vivid dreams she believed were messages from God. In 1849 she escaped alone and walked all the way to freedom.

She could have stayed safe. Instead she went back again and again — thirteen times — to lead about seventy enslaved people, including her parents, to freedom. She said God gave her strength and showed her the way, like the North Star.

Harriet trusted God, not her own bravery, to keep her safe. Just as God had set her free from slavery, she believed anyone could be set free from sin — not by being good enough, but by trusting in Jesus.

name: Jonathan Edwards

significant_dates.details: Born in East Windsor, Connecticut.
Described a conversion experience while at Yale, reading 1 Timothy 1:17 and sensing the glory of God.
Married Sarah Pierpont and was ordained pastor at Northampton, Massachusetts.
Preached "Sinners in the Hands of an Angry God" at Enfield, Connecticut.
Dismissed from his Northampton pulpit over a dispute about church membership; became a missionary at Stockbridge.
Died from complications of a smallpox inoculation, days after being installed as president of the College of New Jersey (Princeton).

footnote: Edwards owned at least six enslaved people during his lifetime, including a woman named Venus purchased in 1731, and in 1741 wrote a pamphlet defending slavery in the American colonies while opposing the Atlantic slave trade. This was a grave moral failure inconsistent with the gospel of grace he preached, and is acknowledged here plainly alongside his theological legacy.
source_summary: Jonathan Edwards (1703–1758) was an American Congregationalist theologian and preacher, widely regarded as one of America's most significant philosophical theologians. Ordained at Northampton, Massachusetts in 1727, his preaching sparked revivals in the 1730s that fed into the First Great Awakening. His 1741 sermon "Sinners in the Hands of an Angry God" remains among the most famous in American history. He was dismissed from Northampton in 1750 over a dispute about communion and church membership standards, after which he served as a missionary to the Housatonic people at Stockbridge. In 1758 he was installed as president of the College of New Jersey (Princeton) and died weeks later from a smallpox inoculation. Edwards was also a slaveholder who defended race-based slavery in print.
adult_story:
Jonathan Edwards was born in East Windsor, Connecticut, in 1703, the only son among eleven children of a Congregationalist minister. He entered Yale College at twelve. For years the Reformed doctrine of predestination struck him as harsh — until 1721, when reading the words "unto the King eternal, immortal, invisible, the only wise God, be honour and glory for ever" brought what he called "a new sense" of God's glory unlike anything he had known before. In 1727 he married Sarah Pierpont and was ordained pastor at Northampton, Massachusetts, succeeding his grandfather.

Edwards became one of colonial America's most significant theologians. His preaching sparked revival in Northampton in the 1730s, an early spark of the First Great Awakening across New England. His 1741 sermon "Sinners in the Hands of an Angry God" remains among the most famous in American history, warning hearers not toward despair but toward mercy freely offered in Christ. His writings defended one conviction: salvation is God's gift from beginning to end, received by faith alone, not secured by human effort.

In 1747 Edwards took the dying missionary [[David Brainerd|david-brainerd]] into his home, sitting with him through his final weeks. After Brainerd's death he edited and published his diary, which went on to inspire generations of missionaries. Dismissed from his pulpit in 1750 over a dispute about church membership, Edwards spent his last years as missionary to the Housatonic people at Stockbridge, dying in 1758 days after being installed as president of the College of New Jersey.
family_story:
Jonathan Edwards was a very smart boy who started college at Yale at only twelve. For years he didn't like the idea that God alone decides who is saved — it seemed unfair. Then one day, reading a Bible verse about God's glory, everything changed. For the first time, he felt how wonderful God is.

Jonathan became a pastor and preacher in Massachusetts. His preaching helped start a great revival, where many people turned to God. He always taught one thing: no one can earn heaven by being good. Salvation is a free gift from God, given to anyone who trusts in Jesus.

When Jonathan lost his church and had to start over as a missionary, he kept teaching that same truth until he died.

name: John G. Paton

significant_dates.details: Born at Braehead, Kirkmahoe, Dumfriesshire, Scotland.
Arrived on the island of Tanna, New Hebrides, to begin mission work.
Forced to flee Tanna amid tribal war and hostility following a measles epidemic.
His translation of the New Testament into the Aniwan language was printed.
Died at Canterbury, Victoria, Australia, aged 82.

source_summary: John Gibson Paton (1824-1907) was a Scottish Reformed Presbyterian missionary to the New Hebrides (now Vanuatu). His first wife and infant son died within a year of arriving on Tanna in 1858; he continued alone under repeated threats to his life. He fled Tanna in 1862, remarried, and from 1866 spent roughly thirty years on Aniwa, reducing the language to writing and completing a New Testament translation in 1899. He undertook extensive fundraising tours across Australia, Britain, and America, and campaigned against the trafficking of Pacific islanders as forced labour.
adult_story:
John Gibson Paton was born in 1824 to a devout farming family in Dumfriesshire, Scotland, where his father kept a ‘prayer closet’ and led the household in worship morning and night. Trained in Glasgow, he was ordained in 1858 and sailed with his young wife, Mary, for the island of Tanna in the New Hebrides (now Vanuatu) — known then for cannibalism and unceasing tribal warfare. Within a year Mary and their infant son were dead of illness, and Paton buried them himself, alone, in a land he had barely learned to call home.

He stayed. For years he lived under constant threat of death — clubbed, shot at, and once held at gunpoint for hours by a chief who eventually lowered his musket. Forced to flee Tanna in 1862 after an epidemic turned islanders against the missionaries, Paton remarried and, from 1866, gave nearly thirty years to the neighbouring island of Aniwa, reducing its language to writing and completing a translation of the New Testament in 1899. He tirelessly campaigned across Australia, Britain, and America, raising support for mission ships and speaking against the trafficking of Pacific islanders as forced labour.

Paton later wrote that in his darkest hours on Tanna, with a gun levelled at his chest, he was sustained not by his own courage but by the nearness of Christ, who had promised never to leave him. It was that promise, not his own endurance, that Paton credited for every soul reached.
family_story: John Paton grew up in Scotland, where his father prayed with the family every morning and night. As an adult, John became a missionary and sailed to a faraway island called Tanna, where people had never heard about Jesus. Soon after he arrived, his wife and baby son both died, and John was left completely alone. But he did not leave. Even when island warriors threatened to kill him, John stayed and kept telling people about Jesus. Years later, on the island of Aniwa, he taught people to read and helped translate the Bible into their language. John always said that when he was most afraid, God was closest to him — trusting Jesus, not being brave, was what carried him through.

name: Abraham Kuyper

significant_dates.details: Born in Maassluis, Netherlands.
Turned from fashionable liberal theology to confessional Reformed faith while pastoring in Beesd, influenced by parishioner Pietje Baltus.
Founded the Vrije Universiteit (Free University) of Amsterdam.
Led the Doleantie secession from the Dutch Reformed Church.
Became Prime Minister of the Netherlands, serving until 1905.
Died in The Hague, Netherlands, aged 83.

source_summary: Abraham Kuyper (1837-1920) was a Dutch Reformed theologian, journalist, and statesman who served as Prime Minister of the Netherlands from 1901 to 1905. He shifted from liberal to confessional Reformed theology during his pastorate at Beesd, later founding the newspaper De Standaard, leading the 1886 Doleantie secession, founding the Vrije Universiteit Amsterdam in 1880, and developing the concept of 'sphere sovereignty.'
adult_story:
Abraham Kuyper was born in 1837 in Maassluis, Holland, the son of a Reformed pastor. Brilliant and ambitious, he earned his doctorate at Leiden in 1862 already leaning toward the fashionable liberal theology of his day, which treated Scripture as human literature rather than God’s word. His first pastorate, in the small village of Beesd, would change that.

There he met Pietje Baltus, a servant girl in his congregation who refused to shake his hand, telling him plainly that he did not yet believe what the Bible actually taught. Her blunt conviction, and his own return to serious study of Scripture, turned Kuyper from fashionable doubt to confident faith in the gospel of grace. He went on to found the newspaper De Standaard, lead a body of churches out of a compromised state denomination in 1886, and establish the Vrije Universiteit of Amsterdam in 1880 to train ministers in that same conviction. He served as Prime Minister of the Netherlands from 1901 to 1905, and in his 1898 lectures at Princeton declared that there is ‘not a square inch’ of creation over which Christ does not claim sovereignty.

Kuyper’s public brilliance was considerable, but he traced its root to a single quiet rebuke from a servant girl who cared more for his soul than his approval — a reminder that saving faith is God’s gift, not the credential of the educated.
family_story: Abraham Kuyper was a very clever man from Holland who became a pastor, a newspaper writer, and even the Prime Minister of his whole country! But when he was young, he didn’t really believe the Bible was true — he thought it was just an old book. Then he met a poor woman in his church named Pietje, who told him honestly that he didn’t yet believe what Jesus taught. Instead of getting angry, Abraham listened. He began reading the Bible seriously again, and this time he truly trusted in Jesus. He always said that Jesus is Lord over every single part of life — school, work, government, everything — because Jesus made it all and died to save us all.

name: Isobel Kuhn

significant_dates.details: Born in Toronto, Ontario, Canada.
After a period of agnosticism at university, prayed for God to prove Himself and give her peace, per her autobiography By Searching.
Heard missionary J. O. Fraser describe the Lisu people's need at a conference in Bellingham, Washington, setting her call to missions.
Married John Becker Kuhn in Kunming, Yunnan Province, China.
Evacuated China over a mountain pass into Burma amid the Communist revolution, ending sixteen years of Lisu ministry there.
Died of cancer in Wheaton, Illinois, USA, aged 55.

source_summary: Isobel Miller Kuhn (1901-1957) was a Canadian missionary to the Lisu people of Yunnan, China, and later northern Thailand, with the China Inland Mission. Converted in 1921 after a personal crisis, she sailed for China in 1928, married fellow missionary John Kuhn in 1929, and from 1934 to 1950 pioneered a 'Rainy Season Bible School' to train Lisu evangelists. She evacuated China in 1950 amid the Communist revolution and continued Lisu ministry in Thailand until illness forced her retirement in 1954. She wrote nine missionary books before her death in 1957.
adult_story:
Isobel Miller Kuhn was born in Toronto in 1901 and raised in a Presbyterian home, but by her last year at the University of British Columbia she had drifted into agnosticism, abandoning prayer and church. In 1921, undone by a broken engagement and at the edge of despair, she prayed a desperate bargain: ‘God, if there be a God... if You will give me peace, I will give You my whole life.’ The peace came, and so did the surrender.

Three years later, hearing missionary J. O. Fraser describe the Lisu people of Yunnan, China, bound by fear of spirits, Isobel knew where that surrendered life was headed. She sailed with the China Inland Mission in 1928 and married fellow missionary John Kuhn the following year in Kunming. From 1934 to 1950 the Kuhns lived among the Lisu, where Isobel pioneered the ‘Rainy Season Bible School,’ timed to the farming calendar so Lisu believers could be trained as evangelists to their own people. When Communist forces swept through in 1950, the family fled over a mountain pass into Burma, leaving behind a growing church. From 1952 she and John continued the same work among Lisu communities in northern Thailand, until illness forced her retirement in 1954. She spent her last years writing books that still carry her testimony that grace, once truly received, cannot be kept to oneself.
family_story: Isobel Kuhn grew up in Canada. When she was at university, she stopped believing in God and felt very lost. One sad Christmas, she prayed a desperate prayer: ‘God, if You are real, give me peace, and I will do anything You ask.’ God answered, and Isobel gave her whole life to Jesus from then on. A few years later she heard about the Lisu people in China, who were afraid of evil spirits and had never heard of Jesus. Isobel became a missionary and moved to China to live among them, teaching them to read and starting a Bible school. Even after she had to flee to Thailand, she kept teaching people about Jesus for the rest of her life.

name: Francis Schaeffer

significant_dates.details: Born in Germantown, Pennsylvania, USA.
Converted to Christianity after months of Bible reading, following a period of philosophical searching.
Ordained as the first minister of the newly formed Bible Presbyterian Church.
Founded L'Abri Fellowship with his wife Edith in Huémoz, Switzerland.
Died of lymphoma in Rochester, Minnesota, USA, aged 72.

source_summary: Francis August Schaeffer (1912-1984) was an American Presbyterian pastor, theologian, and apologist. Converted around age 18, he trained at Westminster and Faith Theological Seminary and was ordained in 1938. In 1948 he moved with his wife Edith to Switzerland, and in 1955 they founded L'Abri Fellowship in Huémoz, offering hospitality and serious engagement with philosophical and religious questions to young travelers. His books, including The God Who Is There, helped shape evangelical engagement with modern philosophy and culture.
adult_story:
Francis Schaeffer was born in 1912 near Philadelphia into a home with little religious interest. As a teenager he began testing the great questions of philosophy against the Bible, reading it cover to cover over several months, and around 1930 he came to trust in Christ. He trained at Westminster and Faith Theological Seminary under scholars committed to the full trustworthiness of Scripture, and in 1938 became the first minister ordained in the newly formed Bible Presbyterian Church.

In 1948 Schaeffer moved with his wife Edith to Switzerland. A few years later, troubled that his own faith had grown more about correct doctrine than a living love for Christ and others, he went through a season of painful self-examination that reshaped his ministry. Out of it came L’Abri (‘The Shelter’), which he and Edith opened in their home in Huémoz in 1955 — a place where the honest questions of doubting students and travelers were met with patient answers and genuine welcome, not dismissal. Through books such as The God Who Is There and How Should We Then Live?, he helped a generation of evangelicals engage seriously with modern culture without abandoning confidence in the gospel.

Schaeffer’s central conviction, tested in his own season of doubt before it appeared in any book, was that Christ alone answers what philosophy cannot: not by argument winning the point, but by grace meeting a person exactly where their questions have left them stranded.
family_story: Francis Schaeffer grew up in America without much interest in God. But as a teenager, he started reading the Bible carefully to see if it was really true — and by the time he finished, he believed in Jesus with his whole heart. He became a pastor, and later moved to Switzerland with his wife, Edith. There, in 1955, they opened their home to any traveler or student with hard questions about God, calling it L'Abri, which means ‘the shelter.’ Instead of turning doubters away, Francis and Edith welcomed them, listened, and pointed them to Jesus. Many people who came looking for answers found something better — they found Christ himself, and put their trust in him.

name: Leon Morris

significant_dates.details: Born in Lithgow, New South Wales, Australia.
Ordained deacon in the Anglican Diocese of Sydney; began curacy at St John's, Campsie.
Published The Apostolic Preaching of the Cross, his landmark defence of penal substitutionary atonement.
Became Principal of Ridley College, Melbourne, a post he held until 1979.
Died in Kew, Melbourne, Australia, aged 92.

source_summary: Leon Lamb Morris (1914-2006) was an Australian Evangelical Anglican priest and New Testament scholar. Ordained in 1938, he earned a PhD at Cambridge and published The Apostolic Preaching of the Cross (1955), a landmark defence of penal substitutionary atonement. He served as Warden of Tyndale House, Cambridge, and Principal of Ridley College, Melbourne (1964-1979), writing over fifty books including a widely used commentary on John's Gospel.
adult_story:
Leon Morris was born in 1914 in Lithgow, New South Wales, and trained as a teacher before entering ministry, ordained a deacon in the Anglican Diocese of Sydney in 1938. Topping the national theology exam and serving first as a curate and then as a ‘bush pastor’ in outback South Australia, he went on to earn a PhD at Cambridge with a dissertation that would become one of the most significant evangelical books of the twentieth century.

That book, The Apostolic Preaching of the Cross (1955), argued — against a rising tide of theological fashion — that the New Testament presents Christ’s death as a real substitution, in which the Son bore the wrath that sinners deserved. Morris’s summary of the doctrine became famous: ‘Either we die, or He dies.’ He served as Warden of Tyndale House, Cambridge, and then for fifteen years as Principal of Ridley College, Melbourne, training generations of Australian clergy, while writing more than fifty books, including a widely used commentary on John’s Gospel, and helping translate the NIV and ESV Bibles.

For Morris, the cross was never a puzzle to be admired from a safe distance. It was the place where God himself, in Christ, paid what sinners could never pay — so that grace, not human effort, is the only ground on which anyone stands before God.
family_story: Leon Morris grew up in Australia and became a teacher before he became a pastor. He studied hard and later earned a very important university degree, writing about something he believed was the heart of the whole Bible: that when Jesus died on the cross, he was taking the punishment that should have been ours. Leon wrote a famous line to explain it simply: ‘Either we die, or He dies.’ He spent his life teaching other people to become pastors in Australia and writing more than fifty books to help people understand the Bible. He wanted everyone to know that we cannot save ourselves — Jesus already did it for us, out of love.

name: Helen Roseveare

significant_dates.details: Born at Haileybury College, Hertfordshire, England.
Converted to Christian faith as a medical student at Cambridge, through the Cambridge Inter-Collegiate Christian Union.
Arrived in the Belgian Congo as a WEC International missionary, establishing a hospital and training centre at Ibambi.
Taken prisoner by rebel forces during the Simba Rebellion and held for several months.
Returned to Congo to found a new medical centre and nursing school at Nyankunde.
Died in Northern Ireland, aged 91.

source_summary: Helen Roseveare (1925-2016) was an English medical doctor and missionary with WEC International in the Belgian Congo. Converted in 1945 while a medical student at Cambridge, she built hospitals and training schools at Ibambi and Nebobongo from 1953. During the 1964 Simba Rebellion she was taken prisoner and suffered violent assault; she later returned to found another medical centre at Nyankunde, serving until 1973, then had a long international speaking and writing ministry.
adult_story:
Helen Roseveare was born in England in 1925 and came to faith in Christ in 1945 while a medical student at Cambridge, through the university’s Christian Union. Trained as a doctor, she sailed for the Belgian Congo in 1953 with the mission agency WEC International, determined to combine medicine with the gospel.

At Ibambi, and later at a former leprosy camp called Nebobongo, she built a hospital and training school from nothing, training generations of Congolese paramedics. In 1964, during the violent Simba Rebellion, Roseveare was taken prisoner by rebel soldiers and held for months, suffering brutal assault. She later said her survival owed much to villagers she had previously cared for, who intervened on her behalf. Remarkably, she returned to Congo in 1966 to found another medical centre at Nyankunde, serving until 1973, and then spent decades travelling and writing about what she had learned of God in the midst of suffering.

One story she told often: a Congolese girl named Ruth prayed simply and specifically for a hot water bottle and a doll, to comfort a dying baby and its grieving young sister — and both arrived, unexpectedly, in a parcel that same day. Roseveare’s whole life testified to that same childlike confidence: that grace, not our strength or worthiness, is what God delights to give those who simply ask.
family_story: Helen Roseveare was a doctor from England who became a missionary in Africa. She built a hospital in the Congo and trained local people to be nurses and doctors too. During a scary war, Helen was captured and treated very badly, but she never stopped trusting God — and some of the very people she had cared for helped save her life. One of her favorite stories was about a girl named Ruth, who prayed for a hot water bottle and a doll for a sick baby. That same day, a parcel arrived with exactly those things inside! Helen's whole life showed that God hears even the smallest prayers, and gives good gifts to those who trust him.

name: F. F. Bruce

significant_dates.details: Born in Elgin, Moray, Scotland, to a Plymouth Brethren family.
Married Betty Davidson, from a Brethren family.
Became Rylands Professor of Biblical Criticism and Exegesis at the University of Manchester, a post held until 1978.
Founded the Christian Brethren Archive at the University of Manchester.
Died of cancer at his home in Buxton, Derbyshire, England, aged 79.

source_summary: Frederick Fyvie Bruce (1910-1990) was a Scottish biblical scholar raised in a Plymouth Brethren family. He taught Greek at Edinburgh and Leeds before leading Biblical History and Literature at Sheffield, then serving as Rylands Professor of Biblical Criticism at Manchester from 1959 to 1978. His 1943 book Are the New Testament Documents Reliable? became a landmark evangelical defence of the New Testament's historical trustworthiness. He wrote nearly fifty books and supervised around fifty doctoral students, remaining a lifelong lay member of his Brethren assembly.
adult_story:
Frederick Fyvie Bruce was born in 1910 in Elgin, Scotland, the son of an itinerant Plymouth Brethren evangelist. Trained in classics at Aberdeen, Cambridge, and Vienna, he might easily have spent his career in secular scholarship — instead he devoted it to demonstrating that the New Testament could withstand the fullest scrutiny of critical history.

Bruce taught Greek at Edinburgh and Leeds before becoming, in 1947, the first head of Biblical History and Literature at Sheffield, and later, from 1959 to 1978, Rylands Professor of Biblical Criticism at Manchester. His 1943 book Are the New Testament Documents Reliable? became one of the most influential evangelical works of the century, arguing that rigorous historical inquiry, honestly pursued, only strengthens confidence in the Gospels rather than undermining them. He wrote nearly fifty books and supervised around fifty doctoral students who went on to shape evangelical biblical scholarship worldwide, while remaining, his whole life, a simple member of his Brethren assembly rather than an ordained clergyman — in keeping with the Brethren conviction that no Christian needs a special office to serve Christ or handle his word.

For Bruce, that conviction was not incidental to his scholarship but its foundation: Scripture's authority rests on what God has done in Christ and preserved in his word, not on the credentials of those who study it.
family_story: F. F. Bruce grew up in Scotland, where his father traveled from town to town preaching about Jesus. F. F. loved studying languages and became one of the best Bible scholars in the world, teaching at important universities in England. He wrote a famous book showing that the stories in the Bible about Jesus can be trusted as true history. Even though he became very respected and clever, F. F. never wanted a special title or position in his church — he believed every Christian, not just pastors, could serve God and study his word. He spent his life helping people trust that the Bible is exactly what it claims to be: true.

name: Martyn Lloyd-Jones

significant_dates.details: Born in Cardiff, Wales.
Began ministry at Bethlehem Chapel, Aberavon, after leaving a promising medical career.
Became associate minister of Westminster Chapel, London, under G. Campbell Morgan.
Addressed the National Assembly of Evangelicals urging separation from doctrinally mixed denominations, publicly opposed by chairman John Stott.
Died in his sleep at Ealing, London, aged 81.

source_summary: David Martyn Lloyd-Jones (1899-1981) was a Welsh preacher and former physician who became one of the most influential Reformed evangelical ministers in twentieth-century Britain. He left a medical career at St Bartholomew's Hospital for ministry in 1927, serving first at Aberavon and then, from 1938, at Westminster Chapel, London, until his 1968 retirement. Known for lengthy expository sermons through books such as Romans and Ephesians, he helped found the Banner of Truth Trust and the London Theological Seminary.
adult_story:
David Martyn Lloyd-Jones was born in Cardiff, Wales, in 1899 and trained as a doctor, rising quickly to become chief clinical assistant to the physician who attended the royal family. But treating patients who faced death with every medical resource available and no lasting hope unsettled him. In his mid-twenties, moved by preaching he heard and by the emptiness he saw in dying rooms that medicine could not touch, he came to faith in Christ and, in 1927, gave up a highly promising medical career to become a minister.

He served first at a small Calvinistic Methodist chapel in Aberavon, Wales, and from 1938 at Westminster Chapel, London, becoming one of the century's most influential preachers. Lloyd-Jones preached for decades through entire books of the Bible — Romans, Ephesians, the Sermon on the Mount — in long, doctrinally rich sermons a colleague called ‘logic on fire,’ insisting that people needed not entertainment but the gospel itself: that sinners are justified before God by faith in Christ alone, not by their own effort. He helped found the Banner of Truth Trust and the London Theological Seminary to keep that same gospel being taught after him.

In 1966 he controversially urged evangelicals to leave denominations that no longer held that gospel clearly — a call publicly opposed by his friend John Stott. Whatever one makes of that disagreement, both men agreed on what mattered most: that the good news of grace, not the shape of any denomination, is what saves.
family_story: Martyn Lloyd-Jones grew up in Wales and trained to be a doctor in London — he was even chosen to help treat very important patients. But he noticed that even the best medical care could not give dying people real hope. This troubled him so much that he put his trust in Jesus, and in 1927 he gave up his medical career to become a preacher instead. He became famous for preaching very long, careful sermons that explained the Bible clearly, especially the truth that we are forgiven not by being good enough, but by trusting in Jesus. For over forty years, thousands of people came to hear him preach in London, learning that the good news of grace matters more than anything else.

name: A. W. Tozer

significant_dates.details: Born near La Jose, Pennsylvania, USA.
Converted in Akron, Ohio, after hearing a street preacher's appeal; prayed alone in his attic.
Became pastor of Southside Alliance Church, Chicago, where he served thirty-one years.
Published The Pursuit of God, his best-known work.
Died of a heart attack in Toronto, Ontario, Canada, aged 66.

source_summary: Aiden Wilson Tozer (1897-1963) was an American pastor, editor, and devotional writer with the Christian and Missionary Alliance. Converted at seventeen in 1915, he entered ministry without formal theological training and served as pastor of Southside Alliance Church in Chicago from 1928 to 1959. His books The Pursuit of God (1948) and The Knowledge of the Holy (1961) remain widely read devotional classics.
adult_story:
Aiden Wilson Tozer was born in rural Pennsylvania in 1897 and grew up in Akron, Ohio, with no particular interest in religion. At seventeen, walking home from work, he passed a street preacher who called out that anyone could be saved simply by praying, ‘God be merciful to me, a sinner.’ Tozer went home, climbed to the attic alone, and did exactly that. He never forgot that the whole of his salvation began with nothing more than a stranger's plain words and his own honest prayer.

He never attended seminary, teaching himself theology instead through relentless reading in libraries and secondhand bookshops. Entering ministry with the Christian and Missionary Alliance in 1919, he became pastor of Southside Alliance Church in Chicago in 1928, where his Sunday evening services drew students from across the city's colleges for thirty-one years. From 1950 he also edited the denomination's magazine. His books, especially The Pursuit of God (1948) and The Knowledge of the Holy (1961), pressed one relentless question on comfortable Christians: did they actually know the God they claimed to worship, or merely know about him?

Tozer's answer always returned to that Akron attic — that nearness to God is not achieved by religious performance but received, like his own conversion, as a gift simply asked for and simply given.
family_story: A. W. Tozer grew up in America without much interest in church. One day, walking home from work at age seventeen, he heard a street preacher say that anyone could be saved just by praying and asking God for mercy. That night, alone in his attic, young Tozer did exactly that — and he never forgot how simple it was. He never went to a special Bible school, but he read and studied on his own and became a pastor and writer. His books taught people not just to know facts about God, but to truly know and love him. Tozer believed that anyone — even a boy alone in an attic — can come to God simply by asking.

name: Oswald Chambers

significant_dates.details: Born in Aberdeen, Scotland.
Married Gertrude 'Biddy' Hobbs.
Became YMCA chaplain to British and ANZAC troops in Zeitoun, near Cairo, Egypt.
Died in Cairo, Egypt, of complications following an appendectomy, aged 43.

source_summary: Oswald Chambers (1874-1917) was a Scottish Baptist-raised Bible teacher associated with the Holiness movement. He founded and led the Bible Training College in Clapham, London, from 1911 to 1915, then served as a YMCA chaplain to British and ANZAC troops in Egypt during the First World War, where he died in 1917 following complications from an appendectomy. His widow Biddy compiled his oral teaching notes into the devotional My Utmost for His Highest, published in 1924, which has never been out of print.
adult_story:
Oswald Chambers was born in Aberdeen, Scotland, in 1874, and baptized at sixteen into a London Baptist chapel. Trained first as an artist and then in theology at Edinburgh and Dunoon, he became a gifted, searching Bible teacher, eventually founding and leading the Bible Training College in Clapham, London, from 1911 to 1915. In 1910 he married Gertrude ‘Biddy’ Hobbs, and their daughter Kathleen was born three years later.

In 1915, with the college's future uncertain amid the First World War, Chambers became a YMCA chaplain to British, Australian, and New Zealand troops stationed near Cairo, Egypt — walking among soldiers awaiting battle, teaching them the same searching faith he had taught his students. In October 1917 he underwent an emergency appendectomy; complications followed, and he died on 15 November 1917, only forty-three years old, with no book to his name.

That might have been the end of his story. Instead, his widow Biddy — who had taken careful shorthand notes of his oral talks for years — spent the following decade shaping them into a book, published in 1924 as My Utmost for His Highest. It has never been out of print since, read daily by millions in dozens of languages. Chambers himself never saw a page of it. His enduring legacy is, quite literally, not his own doing — a fitting shape for a man who taught, above all, that a Christian's usefulness rests entirely on Christ's grace, never on human achievement or length of days.
family_story: Oswald Chambers was a Bible teacher from Scotland who taught students in London and then became a chaplain for soldiers in Egypt during World War 1. He was known for asking people hard, honest questions about following Jesus with their whole hearts. Sadly, Oswald became very sick and died when he was only forty-three years old — he had never written a book. But his wife, Biddy, had carefully written down everything he taught. Years later, she turned her notes into a book called My Utmost for His Highest, which people all over the world still read every single day. Even though Oswald died young, God used his words to help millions of people love Jesus more.

name: Mitsuo Fuchida

significant_dates.details: Born in Katsuragi, Nara Prefecture, Japan.
Commanded the first wave of aircraft in the attack on Pearl Harbor as an Imperial Japanese Navy air commander.
Wounded aboard the carrier Akagi at the Battle of Midway.
Became a Christian after independently reading the Bible, following the testimonies of Peggy Covell and Jacob DeShazer.

footnote: Fuchida commanded the first wave of the December 7, 1941 attack on Pearl Harbor, in which over 2,400 Americans were killed, before his 1949 conversion to Christianity. This is noted here plainly, without minimizing the loss of life or treating his later profession of faith as erasing his role in it.
source_summary: Mitsuo Fuchida (1902-1976) was a Japanese Imperial Navy pilot who commanded the first wave of the Pearl Harbor attack on December 7, 1941, and was wounded at the Battle of Midway in 1942. After the war he learned of the forgiveness shown by missionary's daughter Peggy Covell toward Japanese POWs and read the testimony of former Doolittle Raider Jacob DeShazer, a converted POW. Fuchida became a Christian in September 1949 and spent the rest of his life as an evangelist, touring under the title 'From Pearl Harbor to Calvary' and later befriending DeShazer.
adult_story:
Mitsuo Fuchida was born in 1902 in Nara Prefecture, Japan, and rose through the Imperial Japanese Navy Air Service to become one of its most skilled pilots. On 7 December 1941 he commanded the first wave of aircraft against Pearl Harbor, radioing back the signal confirming surprise had been achieved. He later flew at the Battle of Midway, where he was severely wounded, and served out the war as a staff officer.

Demobilized after Japan's surrender, Fuchida carried the weight of a war that had cost so many lives. In the late 1940s he learned of Peggy Covell, an American whose missionary parents had been killed by Japanese soldiers, yet who chose to care for Japanese prisoners of war out of love for Christ. Soon after, he read the testimony of [[Jacob DeShazer|jacob-deshazer]], an American airman brutally treated as a Japanese prisoner, who had come to faith in Christ in captivity and forgiven his captors. Fuchida began reading the Bible himself — and in September 1949, he became a Christian. He spent his life as an evangelist, touring under the title 'From Pearl Harbor to Calvary,' and became a close friend of [[Jacob DeShazer|jacob-deshazer]], at times preaching alongside people his nation had once fought.

Fuchida never claimed his past could be undone by anything he did. What changed him was not remorse turned into good works, but grace — the same grace he had first seen lived out by people he had every reason to expect would hate him.
family_story: Mitsuo Fuchida was a pilot in Japan's navy during World War Two. In 1941, he led the surprise attack on Pearl Harbor, where many American ships were destroyed and many people died. After the war ended, Fuchida heard about an American woman named Peggy, whose missionary parents had been killed by Japanese soldiers — yet she chose to care for Japanese prisoners instead of hating them. He also read the story of an American pilot who forgave the Japanese soldiers who had hurt him badly. Amazed by this forgiveness, Fuchida began reading the Bible himself, and in 1949 he became a Christian. He spent the rest of his life telling others about the same forgiveness he had received through Jesus.

name: Allen Gardiner

significant_dates.details: Born at the parsonage house, Basildon, Berkshire, England.
Founded the Patagonian Missionary Society in Brighton, later renamed the South American Missionary Society.
Departed Liverpool aboard the Ocean Queen on the final expedition to Tierra del Fuego.
Died of starvation in a cave at Spaniard Harbour, Tierra del Fuego, the last of his seven-man party to die.

source_summary: Allen Francis Gardiner (1794-1851) was a British Royal Navy officer who, after his first wife's death in 1834, turned to missionary work, founding a short-lived mission in Zulu territory before working among Chilean indigenous peoples and attempting repeatedly to reach the Yaghan people of Tierra del Fuego. He founded the Patagonian Missionary Society in 1844. In 1850 he led a final expedition to Tierra del Fuego; stranded without adequate supplies, his entire seven-man party starved to death by September 1851.
adult_story:
Allen Francis Gardiner spent much of his early life as a Royal Navy officer, serving with distinction in the War of 1812. The death of his first wife in 1834 redirected him toward missions: he founded a short-lived station among the Zulu in southern Africa, then turned to South America, working among Chile's indigenous peoples and making his first landing on Tierra del Fuego in 1842. Established missionary societies repeatedly declined to fund so remote and dangerous a field, so in 1844 Gardiner founded his own — the Patagonian Missionary Society, later renamed the South American Missionary Society.

Two further attempts to establish a Tierra del Fuego mission failed. In September 1850, at fifty-six, Gardiner sailed once more, with six companions, for a final attempt. Landing at Picton Island, they found the Yaghan people hostile and relocated to Spaniard Harbour — but most of their ammunition had been left aboard ship, leaving them unable to hunt, and the relief ship never came in time. Over the following months, all seven starved; Gardiner, the last survivor, is believed to have died around 6 September 1851.

When a search ship found the camp, searchers discovered verses from Psalm 62 still painted on the rocks nearby: 'Trust in him at all times... God is a refuge for us.' Gardiner's death, rather than ending the mission, became the very thing that funded and inspired its eventual success — a field watered by grace that outlasted his own strength.
family_story: Allen Gardiner used to be an officer in the British navy, but after his wife died, he decided to spend his life telling people who had never heard of Jesus about him. He tried many times to start a mission to a cold, faraway place called Tierra del Fuego, at the tip of South America. No missionary society would help pay for it, so Gardiner started his own. In 1850 he sailed there with six friends, but they ran out of food, and the supply ship that was supposed to help them never came in time. Sadly, Gardiner and his friends died there. But his story inspired other Christians to continue the mission he started, and it grew for years after he was gone.

name: Katharina von Bora

significant_dates.details: Traditionally said to be born in Saxony; exact date and place undocumented.
Escaped Nimbschen convent hidden among herring barrels in a merchant's wagon.
Married Martin Luther in Wittenberg.
Died in Torgau, Saxony, from injuries sustained fleeing plague and famine.

source_summary: Katharina von Bora (c. 1499–1552) was a former Cistercian nun who escaped her convent in 1523 and married the Reformer Martin Luther in 1525. She ran the Luther household in Wittenberg — farming, brewing, and hosting a constant stream of students and visitors — helping establish the pattern of the Protestant pastor's family. Widowed in 1546, she died in 1552 after being injured fleeing plague in Wittenberg.
adult_story:
Katharina von Bora was born around 1499 in Saxony and, like many daughters of the minor German nobility, was placed in a convent as a child — eventually a Cistercian house at Nimbschen. Reading the reforming writings circulating out of Wittenberg convinced her and eleven other nuns that convent vows could not earn the righteousness [[Martin Luther|martin-luther]] preached as a free gift in Christ.

On Holy Saturday, 1523, the twelve women escaped, smuggled out hidden among herring barrels in a merchant's wagon arranged by Luther. Two years later, in 1525, Katharina married Luther himself. She ran their household in Wittenberg's former monastery — farming, brewing, and keeping boarders — freeing Luther to write and teach, and in doing so helped establish the pattern of the Protestant pastor's family still recognizable today.

Widowed in 1546, she lost much of Luther's estate to legal disputes and fled Wittenberg twice during war and plague, finally dying in 1552 from injuries sustained escaping the second time. She is remembered to have said on her deathbed, “I will stick to Christ as a burr to cloth” — the same confidence in Christ's finished work, not her own record of labor or suffering, that had carried her out of the convent thirty years before.
family_story:
Katharina was sent to live in a convent when she was a little girl. When she grew up, she heard about a teacher named Martin Luther who said God's forgiveness is a free gift — you don't have to earn it by keeping convent rules. She believed it, and one night she and eleven other nuns escaped, hidden in a merchant's wagon among barrels of fish.

Katharina later married Martin Luther. She ran their busy home, growing food and brewing and caring for everyone who visited. Life was often hard for her, but she trusted Christ to the very end. She once said she would “stick to Christ like a burr sticks to cloth” — holding on, no matter what.
