name: Samuel Rutherford

significant_dates.details: Born in Nisbet, Roxburghshire, Scotland; exact date unknown.
Prohibited from ministry and exiled to Aberdeen for nonconformity to episcopal church policy.
Published Lex, Rex, defending constitutional limits on royal power.
Died in St Andrews, Scotland, before a summons on a charge of treason could be carried out.

footnote: Rutherford wrote A Free Disputation Against Pretended Liberty of Conscience (1649), arguing that civil authorities should suppress false religion and explicitly opposing the religious toleration advocated by Roger Williams. This defense of state-enforced conformity is rejected by the great majority of evangelical Christians today, who hold liberty of conscience to be a biblical and moral good. His devotional writing and pastoral suffering remain widely valued.
source_summary: Samuel Rutherford (c. 1600–1661) was a Scottish Presbyterian minister and Covenanter theologian. Barred from ministry and exiled to Aberdeen in 1636 for nonconformity, he wrote most of his famous devotional Letters during that exile. He represented Scotland at the Westminster Assembly and wrote Lex, Rex (1644), a defense of constitutional government that was publicly burned after the Restoration. He died in St Andrews in 1661 while facing a charge of treason.
adult_story:
Samuel Rutherford was born around 1600 in the Scottish Borders and rose to become one of the most learned ministers in the Church of Scotland, serving the parish of Anwoth in Galloway from 1627. His preaching and pastoral visiting were tireless, but his refusal to conform to the bishops' ceremonies brought him under repeated suspicion from the church courts.

In 1636 he was formally barred from ministry and banished to Aberdeen “during the King's pleasure” — an open-ended exile for nonconformity. It was there, cut off from his congregation, that he wrote most of the devotional Letters for which he is remembered: intimate, Christ-absorbed reflections on suffering that Charles Spurgeon later called “the nearest thing to inspiration...in all the writings of mere men.” Restored to ministry after 1638, he went on to represent Scotland at the Westminster Assembly and to write Lex, Rex (1644), a defense of constitutional government that was publicly burned after the Restoration.

Cited to face a charge of treason for that book, Rutherford died in St Andrews in 1661 before the case could proceed, reportedly answering the summons: “I have got a summons already before a superior Judge, and I behove to answer my first summons.” His life's whole direction, like his last recorded words, turned away from his own record toward Christ alone — “Acquainted with Emmanuel's love” is carved on his grave.
family_story:
Samuel Rutherford was a Scottish minister who loved his church so much people said he was “always preaching, always praying, always visiting the sick.” But when he wouldn't do everything the king's bishops demanded, he was sent to a different town, alone, for years.

While away, missing his people, Rutherford wrote letters to comfort them — letters so full of love for Jesus Christ that people still read them centuries later. Even in the hardest years, he trusted God had not forgotten him.

Near the end of his life, powerful men threatened to put him on trial. Rutherford wasn't afraid. He said he had already been summoned by a greater Judge than any of them — and he went gladly to meet Him.

name: Charles Simeon

significant_dates.details: Born in Reading, Berkshire, England.
Converted after preparing for communion at Cambridge, understanding Christ as his substitute for sin.
Appointed vicar of Holy Trinity Church, Cambridge, aged 23.
Helped found the Church Missionary Society.
Died at Cambridge after 54 years as vicar of Holy Trinity.

source_summary: Charles Simeon (1759–1836) was an Anglican evangelical clergyman who served as vicar of Holy Trinity Church, Cambridge, for 54 years despite years of hostility from his own congregation. He became a leading trainer of evangelical clergy and helped found the Church Missionary Society in 1799.
adult_story:
Charles Simeon was born in Reading in 1759 and came to Cambridge as a wealthy, worldly undergraduate at King's College. Ordered to attend communion, and dreading it, he prepared by reading a book on the Lord's Supper — and there, in 1779, understood for the first time that Christ himself had borne his sins as a substitute. “I must lay my sins on the head of my substitute,” he later wrote of that moment.

In 1782, aged just 23, he was appointed vicar of Holy Trinity Church, Cambridge — a post he held for fifty-four years. His own congregation despised him at first: churchwardens locked the pews against him and threw out benches he brought in for the poor, and hostility toward him lingered for years. He answered by starting an extra service for those the church excluded, and simply stayed. Over decades he became one of the most trusted trainers of evangelical clergy in England, mentoring young ministers such as [[Henry Martyn|henry-martyn]], and helped found the Church Missionary Society in 1799.

Simeon never married and gave away much of his income, living simply to the end. He died in 1836, still vicar of the same church, having outlasted every one of his early opponents — proof, he would have said, not of his own persistence but of a Savior who does not let go of those he has bought.
family_story:
Charles Simeon became a church leader in Cambridge, England, when he was only 23. But almost nobody wanted him there — people locked their own pews so he couldn't use them, and threw away the benches he brought in for poor people to sit on.

Charles didn't fight back or leave. He just kept loving his people and preaching the truth about Jesus. Slowly, over many years, hearts changed. He went on to teach hundreds of young preachers how to love Christ and share him with others — including missionaries like Henry Martyn.

He served that same church for fifty-four years, showing that staying faithful for a long time — even when nobody claps for you — matters more than being popular right away.

name: John Charles Ryle

significant_dates.details: Born in Macclesfield, Cheshire, England.
Converted at Oxford after hearing Ephesians 2:8 read in a church service.
Ordained, having abandoned plans for a political career after his father's bank failed.
Became the first Bishop of Liverpool.
Died at Lowestoft, England; buried at All Saints Church, Childwall, Liverpool.

source_summary: John Charles Ryle (1816–1900) was converted at Oxford in 1837, then saw his expected career in politics or business end when his father's bank collapsed in 1841. He was ordained instead, served Suffolk parishes for decades, and became the first Bishop of Liverpool in 1880. He wrote extensively on holiness and Gospel exposition.
adult_story:
John Charles Ryle was born in 1816 into a wealthy banking family and seemed destined for cricket, politics, or business — until two events overturned his plans. At Oxford in the winter of 1837, hearing Ephesians 2:8 read aloud in church, he was struck by the truth that salvation is “by grace... through faith... not of works, lest any man should boast.” Then, in 1841, his father's bank collapsed, wiping out the family fortune and closing off every worldly path he had imagined for himself.

Ryle took holy orders instead, later calling the bank's failure “the greatest earthly blessing” of his life, because it forced him toward the ministry God had actually intended. He served parishes in Suffolk for nearly forty years, writing plainly and forcefully on personal holiness and the authority of Scripture — his Expository Thoughts on the Gospels and Holiness are still read today. In 1880 he became the first Bishop of Liverpool, a new industrial diocese, where he worked to plant churches among the urban poor.

Ryle never treated his ruin as a tragedy to resent. He treated it as evidence that God's plans for a Christian's usefulness often run directly through the collapse of a person's own plans for themselves — and that grace, not a family fortune or a career, was what had truly carried him. He died in 1900.
family_story:
John Charles Ryle grew up rich in England and expected to become a famous cricket player or maybe a politician. Then two things happened: he heard a Bible verse in church that said we're saved by God's grace, not by our own effort — and not long after, his father's bank failed, and the family lost all their money.

Suddenly John's whole future plan was gone. But he became a church minister instead, and later a bishop — helping build churches for poor families in the growing city of Liverpool.

Ryle came to believe that losing his fortune was actually the best thing that ever happened to him. It pushed him toward the life God really wanted him to live.

name: Alexander Whyte

significant_dates.details: Born in Kirriemuir, Scotland, to an unmarried mother; raised in poverty.
Apprenticed to a shoemaker in Kirriemuir at about age 12.
Graduated New College, Edinburgh, and began ministry at Free St John's, Glasgow.
Became colleague and successor to Robert Candlish at Free St George's, Edinburgh.
Died in Buckinghamshire, England; buried at Dean Cemetery, Edinburgh.

source_summary: Alexander Whyte (1836–1921) was born illegitimate and raised in poverty in Kirriemuir, Scotland, apprenticed as a shoemaker before a local minister taught him Latin and Greek. He studied at Aberdeen and New College, Edinburgh, and became minister of Free St George's, Edinburgh, from 1870, known for unsparing preaching on sin and grace. He later served as Principal of New College.
adult_story:
Alexander Whyte was born in 1836 in Kirriemuir, Scotland, to an unmarried mother, and grew up in real poverty — at twelve he was apprenticed to a shoemaker, with no obvious path to further education. A local minister, recognizing his mind, taught him Latin and Greek in his spare hours, opening the way to study at Aberdeen and New College, Edinburgh.

Ordained in the Free Church of Scotland, Whyte began ministry at Free St John's, Glasgow, in 1866, then in 1870 became colleague and successor to Robert Candlish at Free St George's, Edinburgh, where he preached for nearly forty years. He became known for sermons that named sin plainly and specifically — refusing the comfortable vagueness many preachers use — precisely so that grace could be named just as specifically. His studies of John Bunyan's allegories, published as Bunyan Characters, trace this same conviction: that the gospel means little to people who have never been shown, in detail, what they need saving from.

A man raised with every social disadvantage his century could name went on to lead one of Edinburgh's most influential pulpits, later training its next generation of ministers as Principal of New College. Whyte never let his own unlikely rise become the point of the story — his sermons kept turning back to one claim: that grace runs deepest exactly where a person's own record gives them least to boast about. He died in 1921.
family_story:
Alexander Whyte grew up very poor in Scotland. His parents weren't married, which back then meant many people looked down on families like his. At twelve, he had to leave school and become a shoemaker's apprentice.

But a kind minister noticed how smart Alexander was and taught him in his spare time. Eventually Alexander went to university and became a minister himself — leading one of the most important churches in Edinburgh for almost forty years.

Alexander became known for preaching honestly about sin — not to make people feel hopeless, but so they could understand just how much they needed God's grace. His own life proved it: no matter where you start out, God can use you completely.

name: Joseph Parker

significant_dates.details: Born in Hexham, Northumberland, England, the son of a stonemason.
Became minister of the Poultry Chapel, London.
Opened the City Temple, Holborn Viaduct, built under his leadership.
Died in Hampstead, London.

source_summary: Joseph Parker (1830–1902) was an English Congregationalist minister known for extemporaneous preaching, who led the Poultry Chapel congregation and built the City Temple, London, opened in 1874. Described as 'a pillar of orthodoxy,' he had a well-known public rivalry with Charles Spurgeon that later softened into mutual regard. His successor, R. J. Campbell, embraced the theological liberalism Parker himself never taught.
adult_story:
Joseph Parker was born in 1830 in Hexham, Northumberland, the son of a stonemason, with no advantages of wealth or connection. He entered Congregationalist ministry in his early twenties, serving churches in Banbury and Manchester before returning to London in 1869 to lead the historic Poultry Chapel congregation.

Parker's preaching — vivid, extemporaneous, delivered without notes — drew such crowds that his congregation built the City Temple on Holborn Viaduct, opened in 1874, one of the great nonconformist pulpits of Victorian London. He was a contemporary and, at times, public rival of [[Charles Haddon Spurgeon|charles-spurgeon]] — the two sparred in print over points of doctrine and style. Yet Spurgeon later praised Parker's originality generously, and Parker was a guest of honor at Spurgeon's fiftieth birthday celebration, the two having set aside their earlier friction. Historians remember Parker as “a pillar of orthodoxy” — a description that mattered more after his death, when his own successor at City Temple embraced the theological liberalism (“New Theology”) that Parker himself had never taught.

Parker's 25-volume The People's Bible preached through the whole of Scripture verse by verse. Whatever his combative streak in argument, the substance he defended in his years at the City Temple never moved: that the Bible's authority stood, and that sinners are reconciled to God only through Christ. He died in 1902.
family_story:
Joseph Parker was the son of a stonemason in England — nobody special, by the standards of his day. But he became one of London's most exciting preachers, famous for speaking without notes, straight from the heart.

His church, the City Temple, was built because so many people wanted to hear him. He sometimes argued publicly with another famous preacher, Charles Spurgeon — but later the two made up, and Spurgeon even praised him kindly.

Joseph Parker always insisted on one thing, no matter what new ideas became fashionable: that the Bible could be trusted, and that Jesus Christ alone brings people back to God. That never changed, even as the world around him did.

name: R. A. Torrey

significant_dates.details: Born in Hoboken, New Jersey.
Became superintendent of the Bible Institute of the Chicago Evangelization Society (later Moody Bible Institute).
Began serving as an editor of The Fundamentals, defending core Christian doctrines against theological liberalism.
Became founding dean of the Bible Institute of Los Angeles (BIOLA).
Died in Asheville, North Carolina; buried at Montrose Bible Conference, Pennsylvania, which he had founded.

source_summary: R. A. Torrey (1856–1928) was educated at Yale before joining Dwight L. Moody's Chicago ministry in 1889 as superintendent of the Bible institute that became Moody Bible Institute. He led worldwide evangelistic campaigns (1902–1907), became founding dean of BIOLA in 1912, and was a key editor of The Fundamentals, the essay series that gave the fundamentalist movement its name.
adult_story:
Reuben Archer Torrey was born in 1856 in Hoboken, New Jersey, and educated at Yale and Yale Divinity School before further theological study in Germany. He began as a Congregational pastor, but in 1889 was drawn into the orbit of evangelist [[Dwight L. Moody|dwight-l-moody]], becoming superintendent of the Bible institute Moody had founded in Chicago — training laypeople for ministry without requiring the university education few of them could afford.

From 1902 to 1907, Torrey led evangelistic campaigns across the English-speaking world and Asia, often alongside song leader Charles Alexander, drawing crowds that rivaled Moody's own. But his more lasting legacy was institutional rather than personal: he became founding dean of the Bible Institute of Los Angeles (BIOLA) in 1912, and from 1910 served as one of the editors of The Fundamentals — an essay series, distributed free to hundreds of thousands of pastors and workers, that defended the deity of Christ, the authority of Scripture, and salvation through Christ's atoning death against the rising theological liberalism of the day. The word “fundamentalist” itself traces back to this project.

Torrey was as insistent on prayer as on doctrine, writing extensively to urge believers to depend on the Holy Spirit rather than mere technique or personality in evangelism — a caution aimed, not least, at platform evangelists like himself. He died in 1928, having spent a working life making sure that whatever methods changed, the message underneath — Christ crucified and risen for sinners — would not.
family_story:
R. A. Torrey went to Yale University and became a church minister, but the biggest impact of his life came from teaching others. He helped run a Bible school in Chicago that trained ordinary people — not just university graduates — to serve God.

Torrey also traveled the world preaching to huge crowds, and he helped edit a famous set of writings that defended the most important truths of the Christian faith at a time when many were starting to doubt: that Jesus is God, that the Bible can be trusted, and that Christ's death saves sinners.

He always said that only God's power, not clever methods, could really change hearts. He died in 1928, having spent his life pointing people back to Jesus.

name: George Matheson

significant_dates.details: Born in Glasgow, Scotland.
Became totally blind while completing his studies at the University of Glasgow.
Began his first ministry at Innellan, on the Firth of Clyde.
Wrote the hymn 'O Love That Wilt Not Let Me Go' in about five minutes.
Died at North Berwick, Scotland; buried in Glasgow Necropolis.

hymns: O Love That Wilt Not Let Me Go
source_summary: George Matheson (1842–1906) went completely blind while a student at the University of Glasgow, and his fiancée ended their engagement rather than share that future with him. Aided by his sister, he became a Church of Scotland minister at Innellan and then Edinburgh, and in 1882 wrote the hymn 'O Love That Wilt Not Let Me Go' in about five minutes during a time of severe personal suffering he never publicly explained.
adult_story:
George Matheson was born in Glasgow in 1842 and was already losing his sight as a boy; by the time he finished his studies at the University of Glasgow, where he graduated first in his class, he was completely blind. His fiancée, learning the blindness was permanent, ended their engagement rather than share that future with him — a loss Matheson carried the rest of his life, aided afterward mainly by his devoted sister, who learned Hebrew and Greek to help him continue studying for the ministry.

He was licensed to preach in 1866 and served the parish of Innellan from 1868, then St Bernard's, Edinburgh, from 1886. He became a compelling preacher despite — or because of — never seeing his congregation's faces. In 1882, on an evening he later described only as one of “the most severe mental suffering,” he wrote the hymn “O Love That Wilt Not Let Me Go” in about five minutes, without revision. He never publicly explained what suffering prompted it.

The hymn's central image — a love that will not let go, even of someone with nothing left to offer in return — is Matheson's theology in miniature: he had lost his sight and the marriage he had planned, and had nothing to bring God but his need. “O Cross that liftest up my head, I dare not ask to fly from thee,” he wrote in the same hymn. He died in 1906.
family_story:
George Matheson was a brilliant student in Scotland, but he was slowly losing his eyesight — and by the time he finished university, he was completely blind. Even harder, the woman he'd planned to marry decided she couldn't go through life with a blind husband, and left him.

George didn't give up. His sister learned difficult languages to help him keep studying, and he became a minister anyway, preaching for years though he could never see his congregation.

One evening, feeling very sad, George wrote a hymn in just five minutes: “O Love That Wilt Not Let Me Go.” It's still sung today because it says something true for anyone who has lost something precious — that God's love holds tighter than anything we've lost.

name: Christmas Evans

significant_dates.details: Born near Llandysul, Cardiganshire, Wales.
Began working for minister David Davies, who taught him to read during a period of religious revival.
Joined the Baptist denomination and began ministry as an itinerant preacher in Wales.
Died at Swansea while on a preaching tour; buried at Bethesda Chapel.

source_summary: Christmas Evans (1766–1838) was born into poverty in Cardiganshire, Wales, and lost an eye in a youthful brawl. Taught to read as a teenager while working for a Presbyterian minister, he joined the Baptists around 1789 and became a celebrated itinerant preacher across Wales, known for his imaginative and powerful oratory. He died on a preaching tour in Swansea.
adult_story:
Christmas Evans was born on Christmas Day, 1766, near Llandysul in Cardiganshire, Wales — the son the day itself named. His father, a shoemaker, died while Evans was still a child, and he grew up illiterate and desperately poor, working as a farm labourer. As a young man he lost an eye in a brawl, a disfigurement he carried the rest of his life.

At seventeen he went to work for a Presbyterian minister, David Davies, and during a season of religious revival there learned to read, in both Welsh and English, for the first time. He joined the Baptists around 1789 and became an itinerant preacher across Wales, eventually settling for years on Anglesey before later ministries in Caerphilly, Cardiff, and Caernarfon. His preaching became legendary for its imaginative power and physical intensity — contemporaries described sermons that held vast open-air crowds motionless for hours, delivered by a large, one-eyed man who had once had nothing, not even the ability to read the Bible he came to preach.

Evans never let his own transformation — from illiterate, disfigured farmhand to Wales's most famous preacher — become the substance of his message. What he preached, sermon after sermon for nearly fifty years, was simpler and not about himself: that Christ receives sinners exactly as ruined as he had once been, and asks nothing from them but faith. He died in 1838, still on the road, still preaching.
family_story:
Christmas Evans was born on Christmas Day in Wales, which is how he got his name. His family was very poor, and after his father died, nobody taught him to read.

As a teenager, he went to work for a kind minister, and it was there that Christmas finally learned to read, in Welsh and English both. He also lost an eye in a fight when he was young, but it never held him back.

Christmas Evans became one of the greatest preachers Wales has ever known, traveling from town to town so huge crowds came to hear him. He never forgot he had started with nothing — and he spent his life telling people that Jesus welcomes anyone, no matter where they start.

name: Jacob DeShazer

significant_dates.details: Born in West Stayton, Oregon.
Served as bombardier on the Doolittle Raid, bombing Nagoya, Japan.
Read a borrowed Bible during captivity and came to faith in Christ.
Released when American paratroopers liberated his POW camp near Beijing.
Returned to Japan as a Free Methodist missionary with his wife Florence.
Died in Salem, Oregon, aged 95.

source_summary: Jacob DeShazer (1912-2008) was an American bombardier on the Doolittle Raid of April 18, 1942, one of sixteen B-25 crews that bombed Japan following Pearl Harbor. Captured by Japanese forces in China, he endured 40 months of captivity, including 34 months in solitary confinement, severe beatings, and starvation; three of his crewmates were executed and another starved to death. In captivity he obtained a Bible for three weeks in 1944, read it repeatedly, and converted to Christianity. Released in August 1945, he trained at Seattle Pacific College and Asbury Theological Seminary, then returned to Japan in December 1948 as a Free Methodist missionary, serving 30 years until retiring in 1977. His testimony pamphlet, 'I Was a Prisoner of Japan,' was read by former Pearl Harbor attack leader Mitsuo Fuchida and contributed to Fuchida's own 1949 conversion to Christianity.
adult_story:
Jacob DeShazer was born in 1912 in West Stayton, Oregon, and joined the US Army Air Forces before America entered the Second World War. On 18 April 1942 he served as bombardier on one of the sixteen B-25s of the Doolittle Raid, bombing Nagoya in the first American air strike on Japan. Shot down over China, he was captured by Japanese forces and endured forty months in captivity, thirty-four of them in solitary confinement, beaten and starved. Three of his fellow captured airmen were executed and a fourth died of starvation; DeShazer's own death sentence was commuted to life imprisonment.

In 1944 he persuaded a guard to lend him a Bible for three weeks. He read it over and over, and in his cell he came to trust in Christ. Released in August 1945, DeShazer trained at Seattle Pacific College and Asbury Theological Seminary, then returned to Japan in December 1948 as a Free Methodist missionary — to the same nation that had held and tortured him. He served there for thirty years. His testimony pamphlet, 'I Was a Prisoner of Japan,' was read by former Pearl Harbor commander [[Mitsuo Fuchida|mitsuo-fuchida]], and became part of what led Fuchida to Christ; the two former enemies later preached together.

DeShazer never described his transformation as something he achieved through willpower or resolve. It was grace received in a prison cell — undeserved, unearned, and strong enough to turn hatred for his captors into thirty years spent loving their countrymen in Christ's name.
family_story: Jacob DeShazer was an American airman during World War Two. In 1942 he took part in a daring bombing raid over Japan, but his plane crashed and he was captured. For over three years, Japanese soldiers kept him prisoner and treated him very cruelly. One day, a guard let him borrow a Bible for three weeks. Jacob read it again and again, and he decided to trust in Jesus. After the war ended, he went back to Japan — not as a soldier, but as a missionary, to tell the people who had hurt him about God's love. He lived there for thirty years. A man named [[Mitsuo Fuchida|mitsuo-fuchida]], who had led the attack on Pearl Harbor, read Jacob's story and became a Christian too.

name: Louis Zamperini

significant_dates.details: Born in Olean, New York.
B-24 bomber crashed into the Pacific; survived 47 days adrift on a life raft.
Converted to Christianity at Billy Graham's Los Angeles crusade.
Founded Victory Boys Camp to mentor at-risk youth.
Died in Los Angeles, California, of pneumonia, aged 97.

source_summary: Louis Zamperini (1917-2014) was an American distance runner who competed in the 5,000 metres at the 1936 Berlin Olympics. During World War II he served as a bombardier in the US Army Air Forces; his B-24 crashed into the Pacific in May 1943, after which he survived 47 days adrift on a life raft before being captured by Japanese forces. He endured nearly two years as a prisoner of war, suffering severe abuse, notably under camp guard Mutsuhiro Watanabe. After the war he struggled with post-traumatic stress and alcoholism. In 1949 he attended Billy Graham's Los Angeles crusade and converted to Christianity, after which he forgave his former captors. He founded Victory Boys Camp in 1952 to mentor at-risk youth. His story was told in the bestselling biography Unbroken. He died in Los Angeles in 2014, aged 97.
adult_story:
Louis Zamperini was born in 1917 in Olean, New York, and grew up to become a champion distance runner, competing in the 5,000 metres at the 1936 Berlin Olympics. During the Second World War he served as a bombardier in the US Army Air Forces. In May 1943 his B-24 crashed into the Pacific; he survived forty-seven days adrift on a life raft before being captured by Japanese forces. For nearly two years he was held as a prisoner of war, enduring brutal treatment, particularly under a guard named Mutsuhiro Watanabe.

Zamperini returned home from the war outwardly a hero but inwardly consumed by nightmares, rage, and alcoholism. In October 1949 he attended [[Billy Graham|billy-graham]]'s Los Angeles crusade. Graham's preaching on the cross moved him to surrender his life to Christ, and that night his nightmares stopped for good. In 1952 he founded Victory Boys Camp to mentor troubled youth, and spent the rest of his life speaking about the faith and forgiveness that had remade him.

In 1998, at eighty years old, Zamperini returned to Japan hoping to personally forgive his former captors, including Watanabe, who refused to meet him. He forgave him anyway. Zamperini was clear that this forgiveness was not something he had willed into being through sheer determination — it was the same grace he had received at the foot of the cross, now flowing outward to men who had shown him none.
family_story: Louis Zamperini was an Olympic runner who became a soldier in World War Two. When his plane crashed in the ocean, he survived forty-seven days on a life raft before being captured by Japanese soldiers. For nearly two years, he was treated very cruelly as a prisoner. After the war, Louis came home a hero, but inside he was angry and afraid, and he began drinking too much. Then in 1949 he heard a preacher named [[Billy Graham|billy-graham]] talk about Jesus, and Louis gave his life to Christ that night. His nightmares stopped, and he found the strength to forgive the men who had hurt him — even traveling back to Japan years later to tell them so.

name: Chuck Colson

significant_dates.details: Born in Boston, Massachusetts.
Converted to Christianity after reading C. S. Lewis's Mere Christianity.
Pleaded guilty to obstruction of justice in connection with the Watergate scandal.
Founded Prison Fellowship, which grew into the world's largest prison ministry.
Died in Falls Church, Virginia, from complications of a brain hemorrhage.

footnote: In 1994 Colson co-authored 'Evangelicals and Catholics Together' with Richard John Neuhaus, an ecumenical statement affirming shared mission with Roman Catholic leaders. The document drew significant criticism from Reformed and other evangelicals for understating the doctrinal differences — particularly over justification by faith alone — that divide evangelical and Catholic teaching. Colson personally continued to affirm salvation by grace through faith alone throughout his life; this is noted so readers can weigh the ecumenical judgment call alongside his testimony.
source_summary: Charles 'Chuck' Colson (1931-2012) served as Special Counsel to President Richard Nixon from 1969 to 1973, earning a reputation as Nixon's tough 'hatchet man.' Implicated in the Watergate scandal as one of the 'Watergate Seven,' he pleaded guilty in 1974 to obstruction of justice and served seven months in federal prison. Shortly before facing prosecution, a friend gave Colson a copy of C. S. Lewis's Mere Christianity; reading it led to his conversion to evangelical Christianity. After his release, Colson founded Prison Fellowship in 1976, which grew into the world's largest prison ministry, and later Prison Fellowship International. He received the Templeton Prize in 1993 and spent his remaining decades as an evangelical writer, broadcaster, and advocate for prisoners until his death in 2012.
adult_story:
Charles 'Chuck' Colson was born in 1931 in Boston, Massachusetts, and rose to become Special Counsel to President Richard Nixon from 1969 to 1973, earning a reputation as Nixon's toughest and most ruthless aide — his self-described 'hatchet man.'

When the Watergate scandal broke, Colson was named among the 'Watergate Seven' and faced prosecution. Shortly before he was due to appear in court, a friend gave him a copy of C. S. Lewis's Mere Christianity. Reading it, Colson broke down and gave his life to Christ. In June 1974 he pleaded guilty to obstruction of justice and served seven months in federal prison. His conversion was so unexpected that commentators later remarked it was proof Watergate had really happened — no one, they said, could have invented a story in which even Colson turned to Christ.

After his release, Colson founded Prison Fellowship in 1976, and it grew into the world's largest ministry to prisoners and their families. He spent the rest of his life writing, broadcasting, and advocating for those the world had written off — the same grace that had reached him in disgrace, offered freely to men and women in cells much like the one he had occupied. Colson was always careful to say that nothing he built afterward had earned his forgiveness; it had only followed it.
family_story: Chuck Colson worked for the President of the United States, and he was known for being tough and ruthless. But when a big political scandal called Watergate happened, Chuck was caught breaking the law, and he knew he was going to prison. Just before that happened, a friend gave him a book by C. S. Lewis about Jesus. Reading it, Chuck realized he needed to be forgiven, and he gave his life to Christ. He went to prison for seven months. When he came out, instead of going back to his old life, he started a ministry to help prisoners, called Prison Fellowship. It became the largest prison ministry in the world, all because grace found him at his lowest point.

name: Nicky Cruz

significant_dates.details: Born in Las Piedras, Puerto Rico.
Converted to Christianity at a David Wilkerson evangelistic meeting in New York City.
Published his autobiography, Run Baby Run, with Jamie Buckingham.
The Cross and the Switchblade, a film dramatizing his conversion, was released.

footnote: Cruz has ministered within the Assemblies of God and the broader Pentecostal tradition, which holds that believers may receive a subsequent 'baptism in the Holy Spirit,' evidenced by speaking in tongues, distinct from conversion. This is a doctrinal position not shared across all evangelical traditions and is noted here for readers to weigh alongside his testimony.
source_summary: Nicky Cruz was born December 6, 1938, in Las Piedras, Puerto Rico, to a family involved in spiritism. Sent at age 15 to live with a brother in New York City, he ran away and joined the Mau Maus street gang, becoming its warlord and later its president. In 1958 he attended an evangelistic meeting held by Pentecostal preacher David Wilkerson and was converted to Christianity, an event later recounted in Wilkerson's book The Cross and the Switchblade. Cruz went on to attend Bible college, became a preacher, and served under Wilkerson with Teen Challenge, a ministry to addicts and gang members. He wrote his own autobiography, Run Baby Run (1968), and founded an evangelistic ministry now known as Nicky Cruz Outreach. He has since traveled internationally as an evangelist for decades and remains active in ministry.
adult_story:
Nicky Cruz was born on 6 December 1938 in Las Piedras, Puerto Rico, into a family involved in spiritism; his mother once told him he was destined to serve Satan. At fifteen he was sent to live with a brother in New York City, but he ran away and fell in with street gangs, rising within months to become warlord and later president of the Mau Maus, one of Brooklyn's most feared gangs.

In 1958, Cruz went to a rally held by a young Pentecostal preacher named David Wilkerson, who had come to the city specifically to reach gang members like him. Cruz had mocked and threatened Wilkerson before, but that night he broke down and gave his life to Christ. He trained at Bible college, served alongside Wilkerson in Teen Challenge — a ministry to addicts and gang members — and later founded his own evangelistic ministry, Nicky Cruz Outreach. He told his story in the bestselling autobiography Run Baby Run (1968), later adapted into the film The Cross and the Switchblade.

Cruz has spent more than six decades preaching in dozens of countries, always returning to the same point: that the change in his life was not something he worked up in himself. A boy raised to believe he belonged to darkness was met, instead, by a grace he had done nothing to deserve — and it was that grace, not his own resolve, that set him free.
family_story: Nicky Cruz grew up in Puerto Rico in a family that did not know God, and his mother once told him he belonged to the devil. As a teenager in New York City, he joined a dangerous gang and became one of its leaders. Then a young preacher named David Wilkerson came looking for gang members like Nicky, wanting to tell them about Jesus. Nicky threatened him at first, but one night at a meeting, he broke down and asked Jesus to change his life — and Jesus did. Nicky left the gang, went to Bible college, and has spent his life since then traveling the world telling others that no one is too far gone for God's grace to reach them.

name: Bilquis Sheikh

significant_dates.details: Born near Rawalpindi, Punjab, in what is now Pakistan; some records give 1912.
Converted to Christianity after reading the Bible and searching for God.
Fled Pakistan for the United States after persecution and the burning of her home.
Published her memoir, I Dared to Call Him Father.
Died after a stroke; buried in a Christian graveyard in Murree, Pakistan.

footnote: Sheikh's account of her conversion includes a series of dreams and visions that she took as confirmation from God, alongside her reading of Scripture. As with other reported mystical experiences on this site, these are recounted as she described them but are unverifiable, and readers are invited to weigh them with appropriate discernment.
source_summary: Bilquis Sheikh (Wikipedia gives 1919, though the article's own category tag lists 1912-1997) was a wealthy, prominent Pakistani Muslim noblewoman from Rawalpindi/Wah, Punjab, whose husband served as Pakistan's Interior Minister (1962-1965). After asking her chauffeur for a Bible, she began reading it privately, and following contact with missionaries David and Synnove Mitchell, reported a series of dreams and visions that led to her conversion to Christianity on 24 December 1966. After her baptism, her family shunned her, she received threats, and her home was torched. She fled to the United States in 1972, later speaking at churches and Billy Graham Crusades. Her 1978 memoir, I Dared to Call Him Father, sold over 300,000 copies. She died 9 April 1997.
adult_story:
Bilquis Sheikh was born into a prominent Muslim family near Rawalpindi in Punjab, in what is now Pakistan; her husband, General Khalid Masud Sheikh, later served as the country's Interior Minister. Comfortable and well-connected, she nonetheless found herself searching for God beyond the religious duties she had always kept. She asked her Christian chauffeur to bring her a Bible, and reading it privately, she was struck by its picture of God as a Father who could be known personally rather than only obeyed at a distance.

Through contact with missionaries David and Synnøve Mitchell, and after a period of prayer, searching, and dreams she took as confirmation from God, Sheikh committed her life to Christ on 24 December 1966. In the culture she belonged to, the baptism of a Muslim convert was treated as a decisive and public act, and when news of her conversion spread, her own family shunned her as a traitor. She received threats, and her home was eventually burned.

Sheikh fled Pakistan for the United States in 1972, where she spent years speaking in churches and at Billy Graham Crusades about what she had found. Her 1978 memoir, I Dared to Call Him Father, told the story of a woman who had kept every religious rule she knew and still felt distant from God — until she discovered she could simply call him Father, not because she had earned it, but because Christ had made a way for her to be received as a daughter.
family_story: Bilquis Sheikh grew up in a wealthy Muslim family in Pakistan. She followed all her religion's rules, but she still felt far from God. One day she asked her driver to bring her a Bible, and as she read it, she began to believe that Jesus was who he said he was. She prayed and searched for a long time, and on 24 December 1966, she decided to follow Christ. When her family found out, they were furious — they even burned her house down. Bilquis had to leave Pakistan and move to the United States, where she spent the rest of her life telling people that God did not want her to earn his love. He simply wanted her to call him Father.

name: John Knox

significant_dates.details: Born in Giffordgate, Haddington, East Lothian, Scotland; exact year disputed.
Captured at St Andrews Castle; began 19 months as a French galley slave.
Published The First Blast of the Trumpet Against the Monstrous Regiment of Women.
Reformation Parliament adopted the Scots Confession, which Knox helped draft.
Died in Edinburgh, Scotland, aged about 58.

footnote: Knox published "The First Blast of the Trumpet Against the Monstrous Regiment of Women" (1558), arguing that rule by female monarchs was contrary to nature and Scripture. Aimed at Catholic queens Mary I of England and Mary of Guise, the tract nonetheless caused Knox lasting trouble — including with the Protestant Elizabeth I — and reflects a view of women in authority not shared by later Reformed tradition.
source_summary: John Knox (c. 1514–1572) was a Scottish clergyman and the principal leader of the Scottish Reformation. Converted to Protestantism under the influence of George Wishart, he survived 19 months as a French galley slave, studied under Calvin in Geneva, and returned to Scotland in 1559 to help establish the Reformed Kirk, co-drafting the Scots Confession and founding Presbyterianism.
adult_story:
John Knox was born around 1514 near Haddington, Scotland, and trained as a Catholic priest before the preaching of the martyr George Wishart turned him toward reform. When Wishart was arrested, Knox stood ready to guard him with a sword — but was kept back from the execution that followed in 1546. The next year Knox himself was captured at St Andrews Castle and spent nineteen months chained as a French galley slave, suffering for a faith he had barely begun to preach.

After his release, Knox ministered in England and studied under John Calvin in Geneva before returning to Scotland in 1559 to lead its Reformation. His preaching helped topple the old order; he co-drafted the Scots Confession of 1560, which abolished papal authority and the Mass, and he is remembered as the founder of Presbyterianism in Scotland.

Through repeated confrontations with the crown, including several tense audiences with Mary, Queen of Scots, Knox held to one unmoving conviction: that a sinner is justified before God through faith in Christ's finished work alone — not through priest, sacrament, or royal favour. He preached at St Giles' in Edinburgh almost to the day he died in 1572. Fittingly for a man who sought no monument to himself, his grave now lies beneath a car park behind the church he served — his legacy resting not in stone, but in the Reformed faith he helped establish across a nation.
family_story:
John Knox grew up in Scotland long ago and trained to become a priest. But a brave preacher named George Wishart taught him that people are saved by trusting Jesus, not by rituals — and Knox never forgot it.

Soldiers captured Knox and forced him to work as a slave, chained to a ship, for over a year. Even that didn't stop him.

Later, Knox became Scotland's most famous preacher. He told kings and queens, as boldly as he told ordinary people, that everyone needs faith in Jesus to be saved — not good deeds, not titles, not rules.

Knox kept preaching almost until he died in 1572. He wanted people to trust Christ alone, all the way to the end.

name: Nate Saint

significant_dates.details: Born near Hershey, Pennsylvania.
Married Marjorie Farris; the couple opened an MAF mission base at Shell Mera, Ecuador.
Speared by Huaorani warriors at "Palm Beach," Curaray River, Ecuador, during Operation Auca.

source_summary: Nate Saint (1923–1956) was an American missionary pilot with Mission Aviation Fellowship, based at Shell Mera, Ecuador. He pioneered the 'spiraling line' technique for lowering gifts to isolated tribes from a circling aircraft, which enabled the first contact with the Huaorani people. He was killed alongside four fellow missionaries on 8 January 1956 during Operation Auca.
adult_story:
Nate Saint was born in 1923 near Hershey, Pennsylvania. Prevented by a childhood bone condition from completing wartime flight training, he instead put his piloting skill to use for the gospel, joining Mission Aviation Fellowship and, in 1948, opening its jungle base at Shell Mera, Ecuador, with his wife Marjorie.

Saint pioneered the 'spiraling line' technique — circling a small plane tightly enough that a bucket on a long line would hang almost motionless over one spot below — allowing him to lower gifts to isolated tribes who had never seen an outsider survive contact with them. Through these patient, prayerful gift-drops, Saint and four fellow missionaries — [[Ed McCully|ed-mccully]], [[Roger Youderian|roger-youderian]], [[Peter Fleming|peter-fleming]], and [[Jim Elliot|jim-elliot]] — began building trust with the Huaorani people of the Ecuadorian rainforest, a group who had killed every outsider who had approached them before.

On 8 January 1956, having camped for days on a Curaray River sandbar in what appeared to be peaceful contact, Saint and his four companions were speared to death by Huaorani warriors. He was thirty-two.

Saint's death did not end the story. His sister Rachel Saint, and later his own son Steve, went to live among the very people who had killed him — and the gospel he had risked everything to carry took root. Christ's call to lay down one's life for others, he had believed, was not a metaphor.
family_story:
Nate Saint was a pilot who flew supplies to missionaries deep in the Ecuadorian jungle. He invented a clever trick: circling his small plane so a basket on a rope would hang almost still, letting him lower gifts down to a tribe called the Huaorani who had never safely met an outsider.

Nate and four missionary friends — including [[Jim Elliot|jim-elliot]] — spent months slowly building trust this way. But on 8 January 1956, the five men were killed by Huaorani warriors.

It could have looked like the end. Instead, Nate's own sister and later his son went to live among the Huaorani — and many of them came to trust in Jesus, just as Nate had hoped.

name: Ed McCully

significant_dates.details: Born in Iowa.
Withdrew from Marquette University Law School to pursue missionary training instead.
Married Marilou Hobolth.
Speared by Huaorani warriors at "Palm Beach," Curaray River, Ecuador, during Operation Auca.

source_summary: Ed McCully (1927–1956) was an American missionary who withdrew from law school to train for missions after corresponding with his Wheaton College friend Jim Elliot. He served among the Quichua people at Arajuno, Ecuador, and was killed alongside four fellow missionaries on 8 January 1956 during Operation Auca; his body was never recovered.
adult_story:
Ed McCully was born in 1927 in Iowa. At Wheaton College he became close friends with [[Jim Elliot|jim-elliot]], bound by a shared restlessness for missions. McCully went on to law school at Marquette University — but after reading the Old Testament book of Nehemiah and corresponding with Elliot about the claims of Christ on a life, he withdrew the day before his second year was to begin, choosing missionary training instead of a legal career.

He married Marilou Hobolth in 1951, and the couple sailed for Ecuador the following year, serving among the Quichua people at the Arajuno mission station. There McCully joined Elliot, [[Nate Saint|nate-saint]], [[Roger Youderian|roger-youderian]], and [[Peter Fleming|peter-fleming]] in the slow, careful work of trying to reach the Huaorani — a tribe that had killed every outsider who had come near them.

On 8 January 1956, the five men were speared to death on a Curaray River sandbar. McCully's body was recovered downriver by local Quichua Indians but was swept away again before it could be brought back for burial; he alone of the five has no grave.

McCully had given up a promising career for something the world could not see the value of — trusting, as Scripture teaches, that a life is not saved by what it accumulates, but is redeemed only through faith in Christ, who gave His own life first.
family_story:
Ed McCully was studying to become a lawyer when he decided something else mattered more. He wanted to tell people who had never heard about Jesus that God loved them. So he left his studies behind.

Ed married Marilou, and they moved to Ecuador to live among the Quichua people. Later, Ed and four friends — including [[Jim Elliot|jim-elliot]] — tried to become friends with another tribe, the Huaorani, who had never let outsiders get close before.

On 8 January 1956, the five missionaries were killed. It was a sad day. But Ed had always believed that following Jesus mattered more than being safe, or comfortable, or even alive — because Jesus is the one who gives life that never ends.

name: Roger Youderian

significant_dates.details: Born in Sumatra, Montana.
Professed faith in Christ as a WWII paratrooper in England, through an army chaplain.
Arrived in Ecuador to begin missionary work among the Jivaro (Shuar) people.
Speared by Huaorani warriors at "Palm Beach," Curaray River, Ecuador, while radioing for help.

source_summary: Roger Youderian (1924–1956) was an American missionary and former WWII paratrooper who professed faith in Christ in 1944. He served among the Jivaro (Shuar) people at Macuma, Ecuador, from 1953, and was killed alongside four fellow missionaries on 8 January 1956 during Operation Auca, the last of the five to die.
adult_story:
Roger Youderian was born in 1924 in Sumatra, Montana. He served as a US Army paratrooper in the Second World War, fighting at the Battle of the Bulge and in Operation Varsity — and it was in England, through the influence of an army chaplain, that he professed faith in Christ in the autumn of 1944.

After the war, Youderian trained at Northwestern College in Minnesota and married Barbara Orton. In 1953 the couple arrived in Ecuador, where Youderian began missionary work among the Jivaro (Shuar) people at the remote Macuma station — a people known, like the neighbouring Huaorani, for a history of violence toward outsiders.

In January 1956, Youderian joined [[Nate Saint|nate-saint]], [[Ed McCully|ed-mccully]], [[Peter Fleming|peter-fleming]], and [[Jim Elliot|jim-elliot]] in a careful attempt at peaceful contact with the Huaorani. On 8 January, as the group was attacked on a Curaray River sandbar, Youderian was the last of the five to die, killed while trying to radio for help.

A former soldier who had once fought for his country, Youderian had come to believe that the greater battle was a spiritual one, already won at the cross — and that no one, soldier or missionary, is saved by their own courage, but only by faith in the One who fought and died for them.
family_story:
Roger Youderian was a soldier who fought bravely in the Second World War. But during the war, he learned about Jesus and decided to follow him.

After the war, Roger became a missionary instead of a soldier. He and his wife Barbara went to Ecuador to tell the Jivaro people about God's love.

Later, Roger joined four other missionaries — including [[Jim Elliot|jim-elliot]] — trying to reach a tribe called the Huaorani. On 8 January 1956, they were attacked, and Roger was killed while trying to call for help.

Roger had once been brave in battle. Now he was brave for an even bigger reason — because he trusted that Jesus had already won the most important fight of all.

name: Peter Fleming

significant_dates.details: Born in Seattle, Washington.
Sailed for Ecuador with Jim Elliot as a missionary to the Quichua people.
Married Olive Ainslie.
Speared by Huaorani warriors at "Palm Beach," Curaray River, Ecuador, while calling out friendly words.

source_summary: Peter 'Pete' Fleming (1928–1956) was an American missionary and philosophy graduate of the University of Washington who sailed for Ecuador in 1952 with his friend Jim Elliot. He served among the Quichua people at Shandia and was killed alongside four fellow missionaries on 8 January 1956 during Operation Auca.
adult_story:
Peter ('Pete') Fleming was born in 1928 in Seattle, Washington. A gifted student of philosophy at the University of Washington, where he led the university's Christian fellowship, Fleming might have pursued an academic career — but in 1952 he sailed for Ecuador instead, alongside his close friend [[Jim Elliot|jim-elliot]], as a missionary with the Plymouth Brethren agency Christian Missions in Many Lands.

Fleming served among the Quichua people at Shandia, learning their language in the field, and married Olive Ainslie in 1954 after Elliot encouraged him to give his first year of ministry undivided attention. Of the five men who later attempted contact with the Huaorani — Elliot, [[Nate Saint|nate-saint]], [[Ed McCully|ed-mccully]], and [[Roger Youderian|roger-youderian]] among them — Fleming was the only one without children of his own.

On 8 January 1956, as the group was attacked on a Curaray River sandbar, Fleming was the second to die, killed while attempting to call out friendly words in the Huaorani language.

Fleming had exchanged the promise of an academic future for a harder and more hidden calling. He had come to believe, and to live out, the truth Scripture teaches plainly: that a life is not made valuable by what it achieves, but is given its worth entirely by the Christ who redeems it through faith, not through merit.
family_story:
Pete Fleming was very good at school and could have had an easy, comfortable future. Instead, he decided to become a missionary in Ecuador, alongside his friend [[Jim Elliot|jim-elliot]].

Pete lived among the Quichua people and learned their language. He and four other missionaries later tried to become friends with a tribe called the Huaorani.

On 8 January 1956, while trying to speak kind words to the very people attacking them, Pete was killed along with his four friends.

Pete had given up an easy path for a hard one, because he believed that knowing and following Jesus was worth more than anything else he could have chosen instead.

name: Andrew Fuller

significant_dates.details: Born in Wicken, Cambridgeshire, England.
Ordained pastor of Soham Baptist Church, Cambridgeshire.
Published The Gospel Worthy of All Acceptation, opposing hyper-Calvinism.
Co-founded the Baptist Missionary Society at Kettering with William Carey.
Died in Kettering, Northamptonshire, aged 61.

source_summary: Andrew Fuller (1754–1815) was an English Particular Baptist minister and theologian who pastored Kettering Baptist Church from 1782. His 1785 work The Gospel Worthy of All Acceptation rejected hyper-Calvinism, arguing all sinners have a duty to believe the gospel. In 1792 he co-founded the Baptist Missionary Society, serving as its first secretary while his friend William Carey went to India as its first missionary.
adult_story:
Andrew Fuller was born in 1754 in Wicken, Cambridgeshire, England, into a farming family within the Particular Baptist tradition. Converted and called to preach as a young man, he was ordained pastor of Soham Baptist Church in 1775, and from 1782 served the church at Kettering, Northamptonshire, until his death.

Fuller grew up under the shadow of 'hyper-Calvinism' — a teaching, common among Particular Baptists of his day, that reasoned no one should be urged to believe the gospel unless they first showed signs of being among God's elect. Fuller found this crippling, both in his own soul and in his preaching, and in 1785 published The Gospel Worthy of All Acceptation, arguing from Scripture that every sinner, without exception, is commanded and welcome to believe — because salvation is offered by grace, through faith, and not earned by any prior qualification.

That conviction bore fruit directly: in 1792, Fuller helped found the Baptist Missionary Society at Kettering, and became its first secretary, tirelessly raising support so that his friend [[William Carey|william-carey]] could sail for India as its first missionary.

Fuller buried eight of his eleven children and his first wife, and was worn down by decades of unpaid, exhausting labour for the Society. He died in 1815, having spent his life insisting that the gospel is genuinely good news — for everyone who will simply believe it.
family_story:
Andrew Fuller was a preacher in England who noticed something troubling: some churches around him taught that people shouldn't be told to believe in Jesus unless they already seemed 'chosen.' Andrew knew this wasn't what the Bible said. He wrote a book explaining that the good news about Jesus is truly for anyone who will believe it.

Because Andrew believed the gospel was for everyone, everywhere, he helped start a missionary society. He worked hard to send his friend [[William Carey|william-carey]] to India to share the gospel there.

Andrew's own life was full of sorrow — he lost most of his children. But he never stopped believing that Jesus welcomes anyone who comes to him in faith.

name: Ira Sankey

significant_dates.details: Born in Edinburg, Lawrence County, Pennsylvania.
Converted at about age nineteen during a revival meeting at King's Chapel.

First edition of Sacred Songs and Solos published during the Moody-Sankey British campaign.

hymns: A Shelter in the Time of Storm
Who Is on the Lord's Side?
source_summary: Ira Sankey (1840–1908) was an American gospel singer, composer, and hymn-book compiler. Converted around age 19, he was recruited by D.L. Moody in 1870 and served as his song leader until Moody's death in 1899. Sankey compiled Sacred Songs and Solos and composed tunes for hymns including 'The Ninety and Nine.' He went blind from glaucoma by 1903.
adult_story:
Ira Sankey was born in 1840 in Edinburg, Pennsylvania, and came to faith in his late teens at a revival meeting. Years later, as a young YMCA delegate attending a convention in Indianapolis in 1870, Sankey was asked to lead a hymn — and a preacher named [[Dwight L. Moody|dwight-l-moody]], listening nearby, told him afterward, 'I have been looking for you for the last eight years.' After six months of hesitation, Sankey gave up his career to become Moody's song leader, a partnership that would last until Moody's death in 1899.

Sankey's rich voice and simple reed organ accompaniment carried Moody's preaching across Britain and America through the 1870s and 1880s, reaching enormous crowds. He compiled and expanded Sacred Songs and Solos, first published in 1873, which grew to include over a thousand hymns, and composed tunes for gospel songs including 'The Ninety and Nine' and 'A Shelter in the Time of Storm.'

By 1903 Sankey had gone blind from glaucoma, yet he continued serving the church until his death in 1908. His hymns rarely dwelt on his own gifts or losses; again and again they pointed hearers back to a Saviour who seeks and saves those who cannot save themselves — grace offered, as Sankey sang it, to be simply received by faith, not earned by effort.
family_story:
Ira Sankey loved to sing, and as a young man he gave his heart to Jesus. Years later, a famous preacher named [[Dwight L. Moody|dwight-l-moody]] heard Ira sing and said, 'I have been looking for you for the last eight years!'

Ira joined Moody, traveling to huge crowds across America and Britain. Moody would preach, and Ira would sing — songs that reminded everyone listening that Jesus loves them and wants to save them.

Later in his life, Ira became blind. But he kept singing about Jesus anyway, because he knew that trusting Christ, not seeing clearly, was what really mattered most.

hymns: Shall We Gather at the River
Nothing But the Blood of Jesus
Christ Arose! (Low in the Grave He Lay)
source_summary: Robert Lowry (1826–1899) was an American Baptist minister and hymn tune composer who pastored several churches including Hanson Place Baptist Church, Brooklyn, and Park Avenue Baptist Church, Plainfield, NJ. He composed over 500 hymn tunes, most famously 'Shall We Gather at the River' (1864) and 'Nothing But the Blood of Jesus' (1876).
adult_story:
Robert Lowry was born in 1826 in Philadelphia. Ordained into Baptist ministry in 1854, he served a series of churches over the following decades, including Hanson Place Baptist Church in Brooklyn and, later, Park Avenue Baptist Church in Plainfield, New Jersey, alongside a season teaching rhetoric at his alma mater.

Though not formally trained in music, Lowry composed several of the era's most enduring gospel hymn tunes. In 1864, reflecting on the river of life described in the book of Revelation, he wrote 'Shall We Gather at the River' — a hymn he later dismissed, with characteristic modesty, as 'brass band music,' even as congregations everywhere kept singing it. In 1876 he wrote both words and music for 'Nothing But the Blood of Jesus,' a hymn built entirely around a single, unadorned gospel claim: that sinners are cleansed not by their own effort but solely by what Christ accomplished on the cross.

Lowry also encouraged fellow hymn writers, including [[Fanny Crosby|fanny-crosby]], for whom he composed tunes, and Annie Hawks, whose words he set to music for 'I Need Thee Every Hour.' He died in 1899, having spent a working life reminding ordinary congregations, in the simplest possible language, that nothing recommends a sinner to God except the blood of Christ, received by faith.
family_story:
Robert Lowry was a preacher in America who also loved writing music. He wrote the tune for 'Shall We Gather at the River,' a song about the beautiful place the Bible promises to those who trust in Jesus.

He also wrote 'Nothing But the Blood of Jesus' — a song with a simple but important message: nothing we do can make us clean before God, only what Jesus did when he died for us.

Robert helped other hymn writers too, including [[Fanny Crosby|fanny-crosby]]. He spent his whole life writing simple songs with one big message: trust Jesus, because his blood alone can wash away sin.

name: R.C. Sproul

significant_dates.details: Born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania.
Converted to Christ as a student at Westminster College, Pennsylvania.
Founded the Ligonier Valley Study Center in Stahlstown, Pennsylvania.
Co-authored the Chicago Statement on Biblical Inerrancy.
Died in Altamonte Springs, Florida, aged 78.

source_summary: R.C. Sproul (1939–2017) was an American Reformed theologian, pastor, and apologist who founded Ligonier Ministries in 1971. Converted in 1957 at Westminster College, he became one of the most influential popularizers of Reformed theology in modern America, emphasizing the holiness of God, biblical inerrancy, and justification by faith alone.
adult_story:
R.C. Sproul was born in 1939 in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania. As a student at Westminster College, he was challenged by another student's plain, direct witness about Christ, and in 1957 committed his life to him. Sproul later recalled the moment as the beginning of a lifelong pursuit of knowing God more truly.

After theological study, including under G.C. Berkouwer in the Netherlands, Sproul devoted himself to teaching ordinary Christians the doctrines that had gripped him: the holiness and sovereignty of God, the authority of Scripture, and justification by faith alone. In 1971 he founded a small study centre in rural Pennsylvania that grew into Ligonier Ministries, reaching a global audience through books, radio, and recorded teaching. His best-known book, The Holiness of God (1985), retold his own realization that a right view of who God is exposes exactly how little any person could ever deserve God's favour.

In 1978 Sproul helped draft the Chicago Statement on Biblical Inerrancy, defending the trustworthiness of Scripture as the foundation for knowing Christ rightly at all. Until his death in 2017, he insisted that sinners are not saved by their own goodness, however sincere, but by grace alone, through faith alone, in Christ alone — the same conviction that had taken hold of him as a young student decades before.
family_story:
R.C. Sproul was a college student in America when a friend told him plainly about Jesus. R.C. believed, and it changed the whole direction of his life.

R.C. spent the rest of his years teaching people, through books and radio programs, just how great and holy God is — and just how much people need Jesus because of it.

He started a ministry called Ligonier to help ordinary Christians understand what the Bible teaches. He wrote a famous book called The Holiness of God.

R.C.'s whole life kept coming back to one simple truth: no one can earn God's love by being good enough. We can only receive it, as a gift, by trusting Jesus.

name: Billy Sunday

significant_dates.details: Born near Ames, Iowa.
Converted to Christ at the Pacific Garden Mission, Chicago, while a professional baseball player.
Left professional baseball for full-time YMCA and evangelistic work.
Began independent evangelistic campaigns after training under evangelist J. Wilbur Chapman.
Died in Chicago, Illinois, aged 72.

footnote: At several campaign meetings in the 1920s, Sunday accepted public support and financial gifts from Ku Klux Klan members, and his ministry associate publicly praised the Klan on at least one occasion; Sunday is also on record using dehumanizing language toward striking coal miners. These stand in real tension with the gospel he preached and are recorded here plainly, alongside his evangelistic legacy, not to condemn the man but to give a full and honest picture.
source_summary: Billy Sunday (1862–1935) was an American professional baseball player turned evangelist, converted around 1886–87 at Chicago's Pacific Garden Mission. He became the most prominent American revivalist of the early 20th century, preaching an estimated 20,000 sermons to over 100 million people with an athletic, theatrical style that gave rise to the phrase 'hitting the sawdust trail.'
adult_story:
Billy Sunday was born in 1862 near Ames, Iowa, and grew up to become a fast, popular outfielder in Major League Baseball during the 1880s. Around 1886–87, invited into Chicago's Pacific Garden Mission by hymns he remembered from childhood, Sunday committed his life to Christ. In 1891 he turned down a lucrative baseball contract to take a modest job with the YMCA instead.

After several years learning the evangelist's trade as an advance man for preacher J. Wilbur Chapman, Sunday began holding his own campaigns in 1896. His preaching was unlike anything American congregations had seen — athletic, plain-spoken, delivered at a run, often ending with Sunday sliding across the stage as if stealing a base. Over the following decades he preached an estimated twenty thousand sermons to more than one hundred million people, with vast crowds coming forward across the sawdust-covered floors of his temporary wooden tabernacles — an experience that gave rise to the phrase 'hitting the sawdust trail.'

Sunday's central message never changed, however plain its delivery: that no one, however respectable or however far gone, is saved by their own effort, but only by turning from sin to trust in Christ. Days before his death in 1935, his final sermon still asked the same question he had spent his life answering: 'What must I do to be saved?' His answer, then as always, was simply — believe.
family_story:
Billy Sunday used to be a fast, famous baseball player. But one day he heard some old hymns being sung outside a mission in Chicago, and they reminded him of God. Billy decided to follow Jesus — and gave up baseball to do it.

Billy became a preacher instead, and he was very unusual — he ran and jumped around on stage while he preached! Big crowds came to hear him everywhere he went.

But underneath the excitement, Billy's message was always simple: nobody is good enough to save themselves. Everybody needs Jesus. Right up until he died in 1935, Billy kept telling people that Jesus was the only way to be saved.

name: J. Gresham Machen

significant_dates.details: Born in Baltimore, Maryland.
Published Christianity and Liberalism, defending historic Christian orthodoxy.
Founded Westminster Theological Seminary in Philadelphia after leaving Princeton.
Helped found a new Presbyterian denomination, later renamed the Orthodox Presbyterian Church.
Died of pneumonia in Bismarck, North Dakota, aged 55.

source_summary: J. Gresham Machen (1881–1937) was an American Presbyterian New Testament scholar and the leading conservative voice against theological Liberalism at Princeton Theological Seminary in the 1920s. His 1923 book Christianity and Liberalism argued Liberalism was a different religion from historic Christianity. He left Princeton in 1929 to found Westminster Theological Seminary and later helped found the Orthodox Presbyterian Church.
adult_story:
J. Gresham Machen was born in 1881 in Baltimore, Maryland. Educated at Johns Hopkins and Princeton, he spent 1905–06 studying under leading liberal theologians in Germany — an experience that, rather than persuading him, deepened his conviction that historic Christianity and the new theological Liberalism sweeping American seminaries were not variations on the same faith, but two different religions entirely.

Machen taught New Testament at Princeton Theological Seminary from 1906. His 1923 book Christianity and Liberalism argued, plainly and carefully, that the doctrines Liberal theology set aside — the deity of Christ, his atoning death, and salvation by grace through faith rather than human effort or moral improvement — were not incidental but were Christianity itself. Remove them, he wrote, and nothing distinctly Christian remains.

When Princeton's governance was reorganized in 1929 to accommodate more liberal leadership, Machen and several colleagues left to found Westminster Theological Seminary in Philadelphia, determined to keep training ministers in the historic faith. Suspended from Presbyterian ministry in 1935 for his continued opposition to Liberalism in the denomination's mission board, Machen helped establish a new denomination the following year, later renamed the Orthodox Presbyterian Church.

He died suddenly of pneumonia in North Dakota in January 1937, on a winter preaching tour. His gravestone bears a single Greek phrase: 'Faithful unto death' — the conviction, uncompromised to the end, that Christ alone saves, and that this good news is worth defending at any cost.
family_story:
J. Gresham Machen was a teacher in America who loved the true story of Jesus exactly as the Bible tells it. But in his time, many teachers were changing that story — leaving out important parts, like the belief that Jesus is God's Son who died to save us.

Machen thought this mattered enormously. He wrote a book explaining that changing the story wasn't just a small difference — it left out the very heart of the good news.

When his own school stopped supporting him, Machen helped start a new one, so people could keep learning the true gospel. He kept defending that gospel until he died in 1937.

name: George Williams

significant_dates.details: Born in Dulverton, Somerset, England.
Founded the YMCA with eleven fellow drapers above a shop in St Paul's Churchyard, London.
Knighted by Queen Victoria for a lifetime of Christian philanthropy.
Died in Torquay, Devon, England, aged 84.

source_summary: Sir George Williams (1821–1905) was an English draper and evangelical philanthropist who founded the Young Men's Christian Association (YMCA) in 1844 to protect young drapery workers from the moral dangers of city life through Bible study and prayer. He was knighted by Queen Victoria in 1894.
adult_story:
George Williams was born in 1821 in Dulverton, Somerset, England, the youngest of seven sons in a farming family. Converted from Anglicanism to a more evangelical Congregationalist faith as a teenager, he moved to London in 1841 to work as a draper's apprentice — a trade that kept young men working long hours and living together above city shops, often far from any church or family influence.

Concerned for the spiritual welfare of his fellow drapery workers, Williams gathered eleven of them above a shop in St Paul's Churchyard on 6 June 1844 to pray and study Scripture together. That small meeting became the Young Men's Christian Association — the YMCA — an organization built on the conviction that young men adrift in an indifferent city needed, above all else, to know Christ.

Williams went on to become sole owner of his drapery firm and was knighted by Queen Victoria in 1894 for a lifetime of Christian philanthropy, including work with the London City Mission and the Railway Mission. Yet the YMCA always remained his central legacy — a movement that spread worldwide, all traceable back to a handful of young men praying above a shop.

Williams died in 1905, having spent sixty years insisting that the answer to a young man's spiritual danger was never self-improvement, but the grace of Christ, offered freely and received by faith.
family_story:
George Williams moved to London as a young man to work in a shop selling cloth. He noticed that many of the other young workers around him had no church, no family nearby, and no one telling them about Jesus.

So George started a small prayer and Bible study group with his fellow workers. That little group grew and grew — it became the YMCA, which is still around today, all over the world!

George was later made a knight by the Queen for his kindness to others. But he always said the most important thing wasn't the credit he received — it was that young men would come to trust in Jesus.

name: Jeremiah Lanphier

significant_dates.details: Born in Coxsackie, New York.
Began work as a lay missionary for the North Dutch Church in New York City.
Held the first Fulton Street noon prayer meeting; six people attended.
Daily prayer meetings had grown to an estimated ten thousand attendees across Lower Manhattan.
Died, aged 89.

source_summary: Jeremiah Lanphier (1809–1898) was an American lay Christian worker who, as a missionary for the North Dutch Church in Lower Manhattan, began a noon prayer meeting on 23 September 1857 that grew from six attendees to thousands, becoming the catalyst for the 1857–58 Prayer Meeting Revival.
adult_story:
Jeremiah Lanphier was born in 1809 in Coxsackie, New York. After a business as a cloth merchant failed, he became an active, faithful member of several Reformed and Presbyterian congregations in New York City, and in 1857 was hired as a lay missionary for the North Dutch Church in Lower Manhattan — a role with no formal theological training required, only a willingness to serve.

On 23 September 1857, Lanphier held the first of what he intended as a weekly noon prayer meeting for businessmen, distributing handbills inviting passers-by to 'stop 5, 10, or 20 minutes, or the whole time, as your time admits.' Only six people came, arriving late. Within weeks, as a severe financial panic gripped the city, attendance climbed into the dozens, then hundreds; by March 1858 an estimated ten thousand people were gathering daily at linked prayer meetings across Lower Manhattan, and the movement had spread to other cities across America.

Historians would later call this the Prayer Meeting Revival, one of the most significant seasons of spiritual awakening in American history — begun not by a famous preacher, but by one ordinary layman's willingness to pray. Lanphier led the daily meeting for thirty-six years, retiring only in 1893. He died in 1898, having spent his life demonstrating that revival is not manufactured by human skill, but given by God — and simply asked for, in faith, on one's knees.
family_story:
Jeremiah Lanphier didn't have a fancy job or a famous name. He was just an ordinary man working for a church in New York City.

One day in 1857, he decided to start a prayer meeting at lunchtime, right in the middle of the busy city, and invited anyone passing by to stop in and pray. On the very first day, only six people came.

But more and more people kept coming. Soon thousands of people all over the city were gathering every day just to pray. It became known as a great revival — a time when many, many people turned to trust in Jesus.

Jeremiah's small idea reminds us that God can use even the simplest act of faith in surprising ways.
